title: "GNOME Shell"

### Post by Merk42 on 2009-10-29
**What is GNOME Shell?**
GNOME Shell, for those of you that do not know, is a user interface change to GNOME. With it, brings Mutter a combination of Metacity and Clutter, managing the windows. Say goodbye to compiz as it won't be compatible.

GNOME Shell will be [released in September of 2010]("http://mail.gnome.org/archives/devel-announce-list/2009-November/msg00001.html") so it won't be default until at least 10.10, most likely later.

**What does it look like?**
Here is GNOME Shell. 

The date and username provide a calendar and menu respectively
[IMG]http://www.markecurtis.com/etc/gnomeshell/menus.png[/IMG]

Currently some applications are open but minimized. Where are they?
This is the overlay, where you run and manage "Activities"
[IMG]http://www.markecurtis.com/etc/gnomeshell/overlay.png[/IMG]

More applications can be run either by searching for the name in "find", or clicking the bar that says applications
[IMG]http://www.markecurtis.com/etc/gnomeshell/moreapplications.png[/IMG]

You can easily add or subtract workspaces with the + and - buttons. By default, layed out in wall form, the scrollbar, or smaller boxes letting you switch among them
[IMG]http://www.markecurtis.com/etc/gnomeshell/workspacewall.png[/IMG]

You can also view all workspaces at once in a grid layout
[IMG]http://www.markecurtis.com/etc/gnomeshell/workspacegrid.png[/IMG]

Oh look, an instant message! Clicking on the icon in the lower right will bring up the window.
[IMG]http://www.markecurtis.com/etc/gnomeshell/messages.png[/IMG]

Just hover over the message to expand it to view it all.
[IMG]http://www.markecurtis.com/etc/gnomeshell/messageexpand.png[/IMG]


**How do I Learn More?**
[LIST]
[*]Read up on [the design document]("http://www.gnome.org/%7Emccann/shell/design/GNOME_Shell-20091114.pdf").  It gives an idea of where they'd like to go and why.
[*]If that's too much of a read, look at [this brief synopsis]("http://gnomejournal.org/article/85/easy-breezy-beautiful-gnome-shell") on GNOME Shell.
[*]Look at the [GNOME Shell cheat sheet]("http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell/CheatSheet") for features.
[/LIST]

**How do I Try it?**

Running Jaunty, Karmic and Lucid there are three ways to try GNOME Shell
[LIST]
[*]Install the gnome-shell package (easy but out of date)
[*]Use the [Ricotz PPA](http://ubuntu-tweak.com/source/gnome-shell-testing/) (slightly harder, but more up to date)
[*]Build from source (more difficult, but up to date when something is added, provided you rerun jhbuild build)
[/LIST]
Don't try it in a Virtual Machine
[IMG]http://www.markecurtis.com/etc/gnomeshell/virtualbox.png[/IMG]

Building from source is a bit more complicated than the GNOME instructions say. So fire up a terminal
[LIST=1]
[*]sudo apt-get install curl libgstreamer0.10-dev libcroco3-dev xserver-xephyr xulrunner-dev python-dev mesa-common-dev libreadline5-dev libgl1-mesa-dev libwnck-dev librsvg2-dev libgnome-desktop-dev libgnome-menu-dev libffi-dev libgtk2.0-dev libgconf2-dev libdbus-glib-1-dev gtk-doc-tools gnome-common git-core flex bison automake build-essential mesa-utils
[*]curl -O [http://git.gnome.org/browse/gnome-shell/plain/tools/build/gnome-shell-build-setup.sh](http://git.gnome.org/browse/gnome-shell/plain/tools/build/gnome-shell-build-setup.sh)
[*]/bin/bash gnome-shell-build-setup.sh
[*]Get jhbuild (not a command line entry)
[LIST]
[*]For Lucid via repository, package name "jhbuild". You will also need to install "gir-repository-dev"
[*][From Jaunty]("http://packages.ubuntu.com/en/jaunty/jhbuild")
[*][From Debian]("http://packages.debian.org/unstable/devel/jhbuild")
[/LIST]
 
[*]jhbuild build
[/LIST]

To run
[LIST=1]
[*]cd ~/gnome-shell/source/gnome-shell/src
[*]./gnome-shell --replace
[/LIST]

To quit GNOME Shell and return to the panels
[list=1[*]Go to the terminal
[*]hit CTRL-C[/list]

To update (check the [commit log]("http://git.gnome.org/browse/gnome-shell") for anything new)
[LIST]
[*]jhbuild build (rebuilds updated files)
[*]jhbuild build -f -a -c (builds all gnome shell files)
[/LIST]


If you want to make it your default, put "gnome-shell --replace" in your Startup Items

Be aware that there is currently a bug with the cursor being hidden in panel or overlay---the workaround is ALT & F2 to bring up a run dialog & type in "restart"--the shell will restart & grab your cursor.

**Love it? Hate it? Have a suggestion? Make your voice heard in the [GNOME Shell mailing list]("http://mail.gnome.org/mailman/listinfo/gnome-shell-list")**

**Personal thoughts**
I currently have the following issues:
[LIST]
[*]Unable to see what applications I'm currently running at a glance.
[*]Text for running/favorite applications is often truncated
[*][s]Blue dots illustrating number of open windows is often obscured[/s]
(dots and any way of illustrating number of open windows has since been removed all together)
[*]Needless reinvention of notifications
[*]No single click shortcuts available in panel (must go to overlay)
[*]No ability to view/empty trash (the paradigm shift is to remove trash, but much like a lot of things in GNOME Shell they haven't thought of what to replace it with)
[/LIST]


I also wonder if Ubuntu plans on adding its own custom bits to GNOME Shell

---

### Post by collinp on 2009-10-29
Gnome Shell is a fairly radical change for the GNOME project, and it is still in pretty heavy development. It's also a lot more resource intensive for old systems like mine because it *requires* compositing.

I wrote a blog post with my opinion on GNOME shell a while back: [here]("http://scalar.cluenet.org/hellow/2009/10/10/gnome-shell/"). (It also has a few screenshots for those of you that don't know what it looks like)

### Post by knarf on 2009-10-30
As it stands, gnome-shell can not be used on machines which video hardware/drivers which do not support non-power-of-two textures. These machines also can not run compiz, but compiz is optional. If gnome-shell becomes the default gnome window manager these machines can not run default gnome anymore. The Thinkpad T23 I'm currently using is one of those machines. With its 1.2 GHz Pentium III-m is perfectly capable of running gnome. I'm using it as my main machine without any problems.

I should be able to keep running gnome with a non-default window manager but I wonder if gnome-shell is actually worth this sacrifice in compatibility. As far as I'm concerned it is not.

### Post by kestal on 2009-10-30
Though I personally like it, I do not think general users will. If your common general user is in the desktop, I think they'd prefer clicking rather than super-keying and typing. I mean, thats what a GUI is used for, afterall.

Is it productive? yes. Do I personally like it? at 140wpm, sure :) I often have fought for the eye-candy and simplification of things (if needed, instead of being forced to use the terminal - for myself I dont mind, but again, general user).

While I do not say this is set in stone and I am sure there are people who will flock to say things against what I had put in this post, that is just one point of view countering yours. In this thread alone there is enough negative feedback and commenting (which is sad, as gnome-shell is new and different).

I tried it myself and I liked it, but I do not think I am quite ready to get used to it or to replace my current setup. My worry is that, although yes, its an interesting concept which could make ubuntu very unique once polished and added upon (among the other things in store for 10.04/10.10 which I have been reading about that may come to Ubuntu - but thats another topic), I do not think people from Windows will be able to adopt as easily to Ubuntu if Gnome-Shell is used as default.

I am not saying this as any offense to the people behind Gnome-shell, Ubuntu, Windows, gnome (or whoever else you think I may have offended), as I am coming around to gnome-shell slowly.. I guess I will have to see how it ends up design-wise at 10.04/10.10, before anyone can really judge.

### Post by Joe_Bishop on 2009-10-31
> **NCLI said:**
> So, you're still clicking on the button with Start on it to launchprogramsshutdownyourpcopenafolderoraccessyoursettings?

If you are: You're still running Windows
If you are not: You changed your work habits ;)
The main difference is Gnome menu is structured very well, so it's no pain for me to launch the app with it. In fact, launching with menu is simplier than launching with "Super, <few letters here>, Enter". It doesn't seen when you are fresh, but clearly when you are tired. Another pain are these terrible overlay switches, which bring additional complexity to super<....> process: you would be in pain to remember what you wanted to enter before you pressed SUPER. So, the whole gnome-shell idea seems to me completely wrong.

The main point: why do they need overlay? I can't understand why, but it's the thing why the gnome-shell should not be made the upstream stuff. The only nice feature I've noticed is Alt+Tab. I don't like other gnome-shell ideas.

### Post by qamelian on 2009-10-31

### Post by RAOF on 2009-11-01
I quite like many aspects of gnome-shell.  Particularly:
[list]
[*] For me, the overlay is an even-better version of compiz's *scale* plugin, which was the most important thing to happen to window management in many years.
[*] Application-based alt-tab is a great idea, although the actual implementation could be tweaked significantly.
[*] The notifications ideas seem pretty good, particularly the notification log.
[/list]

There are also things I find problematic, such as:
[list]
[*] Why is the shell built into the window manager?  Why *can't* I use compiz with the new shell?
[*] Why are the new notifications a totally new set of interfaces?
[*] Panel applets can be pretty useful - what is the replacement?
[/list]

---

### Post by autocrosser on 2009-11-01
I really want 3 things--

1. A way to configure the upper panel the way I want--In my current setup, I don't use a top panel at all--I came from the Mac OS world & helped Beta OSX--So you might guess that I still do a OSX-like dock at the bottom......(Screenshot)

2. A way to make the panel non-solid colour--I like a desktop that blends well & only intrudes in a minimal manner----a simple slider like it is at present would be nice.

3. A way to easily shut off composite--I game & it's a real pain to turn it off in the Shell--you really need to quit the Shell, play your game & then turn the Shell back on--messy.

All-in-all, I like the way it is going & have had some very good conversations with the developers--nice change from the "normal" Gnome practice..........

### Post by *g!t5^_)*(H on 2009-11-01
>  Originally Posted by NCLI  View Post
So, you're still clicking on the button with Start on it to launchprogramsshutdownyourpcopenafolderoraccessyou rsettings?

If you are: You're still running Windows
If you are not: You changed your work habits 

'Start' button or 'Gnome top menu' are visible on the screen, 'Super' key is another key mixed with many others in the keyboard and nobody knows it can be useful to start a new kind of 'start menu'.

### Post by Keyper7 on 2009-11-05
What I like: it's pretty close to how I *already* use my computer. I currently abuse of Expo, Scale, hotcorners and Gnome-Do and maintain a minimal panel.

What I'm afraid of:

- Compositing required. I have the required hardware, but what about powersaving when I'm on battery? It's clumsy enough that today I have to manually turn off Compiz when I pull the plug (KDE is much better in this regard, as KWin can be set to automatically turn compositing off when you're on batteries), but not having an option to turn compositing off at all? Heck, even Vista have a non-composited fallback mode.

- The end of applets. This is not decided yet, but I'm afraid of the possibility. I use a minimal panel but not THAT minimal.

- Clash of interests between Gnome and Ubuntu. Regardless of how good gnome-shell turns out, Canonical might prioritize familiarity. Plus, the Gnome team is rethinking messaging and notifications, the same goals of the Ayatana project. If they start to go in opposite directions, things might get ugly.

### Post by RAOF on 2009-11-08
> **Keyper7 said:**
> ...
- Compositing required. I have the required hardware, but what about powersaving when I'm on battery? It's clumsy enough that today I have to manually turn off Compiz when I pull the plug (KDE is much better in this regard, as KWin can be set to automatically turn compositing off when you're on batteries), but not having an option to turn compositing off at all? Heck, even Vista have a non-composited fallback mode.
...

While requiring OpenGL (and a working GL_EXT_texture_from_pixmap) has problems, I don't think power consumption is one of them.  All of the actual data I've seen show essentially no power consumption difference Compiz vs (non-composited) Metacity.

While the GPU core can undoubtedly consume lots of power, the typical demands of desktop compositing appear not to stress the GPU enough to make a noticeable change in power consumption.  And in not-too-uncommon circumstances compositing will reduce the amount of computation required, which obviously conserves power.

---

### Post by RAOF on 2009-11-09
> **Starks said:**
> Will GNOME Shell have a fallback mode for older chipsets?

Last I heard, the answer was "no".  The shell's interface design assumes compositing & fast transforms, particularly the overlay, and while it would be technically possible to do (almost?) all of it with XRender, there's little point.  If a card doesn't have drivers that do decent 3D, there's very little chance[1] that their drivers do enough 2D acceleration to make gnome-shell viable.

I don't think that they've built in a backend abstraction, like KWin has.

[1] The current exception to this being nouveau, which has no (supported) 3D and pretty much the best 2D acceleration of any X driver *and* runs on beefy GPUs.

### Post by RAOF on 2009-11-09
> **Joe_Bishop said:**
> Are you sure, or you are just "think"? Proof please.

I'm not sure; that came from my memory of reading some of the early gnome-desktop-devel threads about the design of gnome-shell.  They may have decided to change, but I doubt it.

> **Joe_Bishop said:**
> Nouveau 2D only faster than nv, but not the proprietary nvidia driver. You seem  to believe in what you are spoken, not to speak about you believe in.

My GeForce 6600 and GeForce 7600GO beg to differ.  Nouveau is faster at 2D (and uses less CPU) than the binary nvidia drivers for my cards.  Your mileage may, of course, vary; these nv4x chips are some of the best supported by nouveau, although I think nv5x & nv6x chips may be beating them out now.

> **tawmas said:**
> Out of sheer ignorance and curiosity, could you please explain what those not-too-uncommon circumstances are (or point me toward something I can read by myself)?

When (a) the window contents don't change often, and (b) you expose part of a previously hidden window, either by moving a window or by alt-tabbing.  In the composited-case, this doesn't require any additional computation - the window is already there, in graphics memory, from when you last looked at it.  In the non-composited case, this requires the application to re-draw its window, which can require a surprising amount of computation.

---

I run gnome-shell from git with twinview using proprietary nvidia drivers (8800GTS), and I start it automatically by symlinking the file

to  ~/.config/autostart


rather than using --replace

Maybe the issue is driver problem with SLI, have you tried without?

### Post by autocrosser on 2009-11-18
Enable your "hidden items" in Nautilus & goto .config---if there isn't a autostart folder inside .config, just create one & put the link to the gnome-shell.desktop inside it....

The autostart folder is created when you add startup items to your session, so it might not be there--it's not created by default. System>Preferences>Startup Application Preferences>Startup Programs tab.

Bob--I tried software cursor & almost b0rked gnome-shell--it did not like it at all.....still have a hidden cursor in session--I'll have to do some digging on that issue......

### Post by Roasted on 2009-11-18
So I got the time to spend a few hours playing with it tonight. I clicked around and tried to get the feel of what the developers were doing with it and tried to get the jist of everything.

I mean, just today I caught wind that my boss was warming up to Ubuntu more and more. And I was almost saddened. I just think anybody in a business situation would be disappointed if Ubuntu, the distro everybody knows about as being one of the most user friendly, has an interface that looks like that. I just can't see it being applied to a production environment that easily.

But granted, I have to be fair. I have a lot of faith in the Gnome developers and I'm sure they can whip something real nice up. But I truly, truly hope the rumors I've been reading are false, in regard to Gnome dropping all panels and using ONLY the shell. I really think a lot of users might turn away and drop it cold turkey if they don't have the option to switch back. ### Post by autocrosser on 2009-11-19
Well--the thing that I will point out is that I am VERY sure as soon as a "finalized" format comes out there will be people hacking it in all kinds of ways--And I would really bet that mods/config GUI & howto sites/blogs will grow like the weeds in my backyard :)

I did however find that this next gen interface was horribly flawed in actual use for me. I got to the point where when I was trying to design it in my head it came to borrowing ideas from the shell to make it functional. I still think moblin e.g. has communications much more right than g-s currently but I have a feeling that it will be easier to fix that in g-s than fixing the multiple other things that are wrong with moblin.

That way I can focus on testing the software rather than building it.

### Post by Peter09 on 2009-11-20
Well I've been using it regularly on three machines, a dual monitor desktop, a laptop and an aspire 1 netbook. I find it to be superior (given that it is still in development) to the normal gnome system. There are still rough edges but the ability to switch across multiple desktops and find active windows is superior. Also, especially for the netbook, the amount of usable desktop is much greater due to the ease of switching between workspaces and also creating new workspaces. I can put apps such as firefox and thunderbird full screen.

 At the moment the major weakness is starting an application, having to resort to the keyboard is not always an easy option, especially when you are using the mouse intensely, as it requires either to drop the mouse or type one handed.

### Post by autocrosser on 2009-11-20
> **Tompalaz said:**
> I'm sorry, I've lost the thread where there is a description how I can run G-S from start and don't need to run gnome-shell --replace.
Do you know how I can do this?

Two ways---

1. Put  "gnome-shell --replace" in your Startup Items

2. Simlink /gnome-shell/install/share/applications/gnome-shell.desktop to your /.config/autostart folder

Be aware that there is currently a bug with the cursor being hidden in panel or overlay---the workaround is ALT & F2 to bring up a run dialog & type in "restart"--the shell will restart & grab your cursor.

---

2. Simlink /gnome-shell/install/share/applications/gnome-shell.desktop to your /.config/autostart folder

Be aware that there is currently a bug with the cursor being hidden in panel or overlay---the workaround is ALT & F2 to bring up a run dialog & type in "restart"--the shell will restart & grab your cursor.

### Post by RAOF on 2009-11-20
> **MacUntu said:**
> So how do I run this with "vesa" or "nv" video driver?

> **TrueTom said:**
> You can't. But according to the Gnome Shell developers this isn't a problem because every computer shiped in the last five years has working 3D drivers that never fail.

I think this is quite a reasonable decision.  This is on a par with "How do you run Compiz with the vesa or nv drivers?" - Ubuntu's out-of-the-box experience has been superior for those with hardware supported by open-source drivers for some time.

When gnome-shell becomes default, people without 3D support will get the current desktop.  People with 3D support will get the shiny new shell.

> **t.rei said:**
> I'm wondering... how is this usable after a fresh install then? 
The 'proper' drivers can't be shipped due to legal reasons, right?

For Intel & (many) ATi users, it's usable out of the box right now; the open source drivers *are* the "proper" drivers.  For nVidia hardware... the nouveau drivers will eventually take over the default install.  Probably not for lucid, but I'd be surprised if they're not default in lucid+1.  They should also reasonably support gnome-shell by then - they currently close-to-work on my nv4x card, and I think nv5x 3D support is now superior.

### Post by Roasted on 2009-11-21
After using Gnome Shell more extensively, I'll admit I can see how it's relatively practical to use, but as others have stated - it needs a lot of work yet.

There's a couple things at first glance I don't like that I would like to see integrated, so I'm posting them here in hopes of generating some sort of "yeah I agree" or "bad idea roasted" opinions.

When you click the Activities button and you see all of your windows in thumbnail format, we should be able to delete the active windows from there. I would like to be able to right click on that thumbnail of that program and have the option to close it. That would make managing open applications so much easier since I could just see what's all running and right click - close, right click - close, right click - close, and bam I have all of my unused applications closed.

And one thing that is a very small pet peeve that I would like to see changed is the "more" button for applications is kind of small. Look at all of the empty space in between the actual word "Applications" and the word "More". Let's utilize that space a little more. Let's chop it - 50/50. Left side is not a button, just a section to say APPLICATIONS. Right side is a bigger button spanning across the entire right side to display "More." 

I'd also like to have the option to expand to the right the options in the Places menu. When you hit more for applications, your available applications kick to the right. I'd like to be able to right click and have a menu kick out to the right with the contents of "pictures" or "music" or whatever I right clicked. That would allow the single left click to still be the real controller on launching that window in full screen. I think that option would be a nice one to have.

And lastly, that top panel - it needs to be customizable. I want to add my system monitor there. I want to see what my activity is on my computer as I use it since it also doubles as a backup/file server. I want to add some simple widgets there for my own customizable purposes. 

So there you have it. I said from the beginning I have faith in the developers and I'm sure they'll whip up something good. Despite the fact I gave it some ****, I spent more and more time trying it out and I just sort of "got it" after a little bit. If a few things are changed I'll be an even bigger fan of it. Now to report my findings to the devs. :popcorn:

---

### Post by autocrosser on 2009-11-21
I was just posting to the developer-list & saw your post, so I hope you don't mind that I included it with things I was talking about....You make several very valid points & I wanted to get them in for the developers to look at....The post looks like:

> I am having the same problem with the nvidia proprietary drivers. Redraw is slow & moving windows lags behind the cursor---this with a SLI dual 9800GT set, so I do have more than enough GPU power to really accelerate anything onscreen. 

I also like the ideas put forward about window placement/resizing--

[INDENT]  *Suggestion:*
My suggestion would allow users to draw boxes on their screen (which would
snap to window edges / other boxes), once set the borders could be dragged
and resized or the box removed entirely. Windows could then be dragged in to
the box and if dragged near the top edge they would automatically fill the
box. Alternatively you could put an application in the middle of the box and
then when you use the maximize functionality it would maximize
the application to the box rather than the full window. Boxes could be a bit
unsightly so they wouldn't be visible unless the user is currently dragging
a window, hovering over the maximize icon or if they are in the box setup
system.
[/INDENT] 
 In the thread on ubuntuforums I read this:

[INDENT]When you click the Activities button and you see all of your windows in thumbnail format, we should be able to delete the active windows from there. I would like to be able to right click on that thumbnail of that program and have the option to close it. That would make managing open applications so much easier since I could just see what's all running and right click - close, right click - close, right click - close, and bam I have all of my unused applications closed.

And one thing that is a very small pet peeve that I would like to see changed is the "more" button for applications is kind of small. Look at all of the empty space in between the actual word "Applications" and the word "More". Let's utilize that space a little more. Let's chop it - 50/50. Left side is not a button, just a section to say APPLICATIONS. Right side is a bigger button spanning across the entire right side to display "More." 

I'd also like to have the option to expand to the right the options in the Places menu. When you hit more for applications, your available applications kick to the right. I'd like to be able to right click and have a menu kick out to the right with the contents of "pictures" or "music" or whatever I right clicked. That would allow the single left click to still be the real controller on launching that window in full screen. I think that option would be a nice one to have.

And lastly, that top panel - it needs to be customizable. I want to add my system monitor there. I want to see what my activity is on my computer as I use it since it also doubles as a backup/file server. I want to add some simple widgets there for my own customizable purposes.  (this comment from ubuntuforums member: roasted)
[/INDENT]

### Post by autocrosser on 2009-11-21
> **Merk42 said:**
> I'm going to work on updating the OP. so I just want to check some things first.
If I build from source, do I have delete ~/gnome-shell and rerun jhbuild build to keep up to update?

No--there are two ways to update from source--

```
dean@linux:~/Desktop$ curl -O http://git.gnome.org/cgit/gnome-shell/plain/tools/build/gnome-shell-build-setup.sh

dean@linux:~/Desktop$ /bin/bash gnome-shell-build-setup.sh

dean@linux:~/Desktop$ jhbuild build -f -a -c

Or just:

dean@linux:~/Desktop$ jhbuild build


```The difference between jhbuild build & jhbuild build -f -a -c is that the latter rebuilds all installed Gnome-shell parts everytime--if they need it or not--whilst jhbuild build only rebuilds the updated/changed parts.....the second command is useful if the shell session b0rks or has a problem....I rerun all three commands every time in case there is a change to the build-setup script or to jhbuild itself....

Jhbuild was removed from the repositories because it was a severely outdated snapshot. Removing it was better than keeping it since it was causing many problems due to incompatibility and unfixed problems.

More information can be found in [bug #419363]("https://launchpad.net/bugs/419363").

I haven't tested GNOME Shell thoroughly yet, but the idea doesn't look that bad.
However, the fact that 3D support is a requirement does worry me. Not all hardware supports it, a selling point is that Ubuntu runs on older computers as well and not all 3D drivers work flawlessly or keep supporting old devices.
And what about the corporate users? Would they really enjoy GNOME Shell?

### Post by autocrosser on 2009-11-22
OK--I just was talking to another Gnome-Shell person & a thought came up--My xsession.errors log was being flooded with a file lookup & he commented that it looked like Metacity was trying to restart on a regular basis....I have been starting my session with a simlink to gnome-shell.desktop, so I changed to to just "gnome-shell --replace" and the remaining lag I had is almost totally gone......

So the recommended way to start the shell as a session in Ubuntu is with the gnome-shell --replace command in your startup programs...Not sure why this is a problem--people I've talked to with Fedora are not having this problem.....

So Merk42--you might redo the OP to reflect this, as it will give a better first impression of the shell...(and yes, I know some on this thread just don't like it anyway....), but it is nice to have some "real" idea of how the shell is working at this time....without having a problem that is "self-induced" & not easily found.....

### Post by ibuclaw on 2009-11-22
> **autocrosser said:**
> OK--I just was talking to another Gnome-Shell person & a thought came up--My xsession.errors log was being flooded with a file lookup & he commented that it looked like Metacity was trying to restart on a regular basis....I have been starting my session with a simlink to gnome-shell.desktop, so I changed to to just "gnome-shell --replace" and the remaining lag I had is almost totally gone......

So the recommended way to start the shell as a session in Ubuntu is with the gnome-shell --replace command in your startup programs...Not sure why this is a problem--people I've talked to with Fedora are not having this problem.....

So Merk42--you might redo the OP to reflect this, as it will give a better first impression of the shell...(and yes, I know some on this thread just don't like it anyway....), but it is nice to have some "real" idea of how the shell is working at this time....without having a problem that is "self-induced" & not easily found.....

Remove Gnome-Shell from your AutoStart programs... You are doing it wrong.

Open up gconf-editor (Press Alt+F2 and type it in).
Then browse to:
```
/desktop/gnome/session/required_components
```
And change the value of the "**windowmanager**" key to:
```
gnome-shell
```
This is how you **should** be doing it.

Regards
Iain

> [https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=602766](https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=602766)
  gnome-shell | general | unspecified

           Summary: Activities should also be a task switcher
    Classification: Other
           Product: gnome-shell
           Version: unspecified
        OS/Version: Linux
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: enhancement
          Priority: Normal
         Component: general
        AssignedTo: [EMAIL="gnome-shell-maint@gnome.bugs"]gnome-shell-maint@gnome.bugs[/EMAIL]
        ReportedBy: [EMAIL="francis.earl@gmail.com"]francis.earl@gmail.com[/EMAIL]
         QAContact: [EMAIL="gnome-shell-maint@gnome.bugs"]gnome-shell-maint@gnome.bugs[/EMAIL]
      GNOME target: ---
     GNOME version: ---


For instance, when using gEdit, when I click on the gEdit icon in the panel
section, it should have a similar drop-down to the clock widget allowing me to
choose any application that is currently open.

I don't think different windows for each app should be used, just one entry
like the grouping in gnome-panel of all windows for one app, but it should
definitely be able to do something. One of the first things I did when I saw
this widget was to click on it, and was somewhat surprised that its just a
space filler...I believe I will add to the report when I get home---you might be interested in doing so also......If your shell install is lacking this widget--tell me & I'll do a screenshot of it......

### Post by macvr on 2009-11-24
> **autocrosser said:**
> Well--I tried it that way & it did not work---Changed as suggested & restarted--got a normal session with upper & lower panels---as soon as I changed all back I got my gnome-shell session.

Using "gnome-shell --replace" in Sessions was suggested by Colin Walters, who I think, knows a bit more than either of us.......


To start a session using gnome-shell you need to :
> You can run ‘gconftool-2—set /desktop/gnome/session/required_components/windowmanager—type string gnome-shell’ to always start GNOME Shell when you log in. 
You can run ‘gconftool-2—unset /desktop/gnome/session/required_components/windowmanager’ to reverse this change.

From > [http://gnomejournal.org/article/85/easy-breezy-beautiful-gnome-shell]("http://gnomejournal.org/article/85/easy-breezy-beautiful-gnome-shell")

Merk42 , can you add that^ to the first post.

---

### Post by autocrosser on 2009-11-24
> **macvr said:**
> To start a session using gnome-shell you need to :


From > [http://gnomejournal.org/article/85/easy-breezy-beautiful-gnome-shell](http://gnomejournal.org/article/85/easy-breezy-beautiful-gnome-shell)

Merk42 , can you add that^ to the first post.

Yes--that is the way IF you install from a repo---that way WILL NOT work if you build from source---that way works with the gnome-shell --replace in your Startup.

It is also strongly recommended to remove "gnome-panel" from the required_components/panel section also.

---

### Post by macvr on 2009-11-24
> **autocrosser said:**
> Yes--that is the way IF you install from a repo---that way WILL NOT work if you build from source---that way works with the gnome-shell --replace in your Startup.

### Post by PRGUY85 on 2009-11-24
I currently am using Gnome-Shell as my default window environment and,for a work in progress, I'm quite pleased.  It allows for better use of workspaces and allows me to use more shortcuts to speed up stuff.  It has a Gnome-Do quality in hitting the Windows key and placing the cursor on the Applications search field directly.  This allows for launching applications without ever leaving the keyboard.  I was impressed with the new Alt-Tab additions with showing progresses for the same application as an organized sub-tree.

Will continue to use and report.  I am currently not using any Dock or Gnome-Do, just Gnome-Shell.

### Post by autocrosser on 2009-11-25
Not that I know of---Glad that one of the ways worked for you---don't quite understand why jhbuild was b0rking---but as long as you've got something to test...that's good....

Note for general info:  If you want a better amount or choice of applications in your "Activities>Applications" list---run gconf-editor, tip open "desktop">"gnome">"shell"--highlight shell & edit favorite_apps---remember to use the "real" name for the app--followed with .desktop.  Close & check that your app(s) are listed the way you want---The default is wrong in the source-built shell for Firefox---listed as "mozilla-firefox.desktop"---we just use "firefox.desktop"

### Post by Merk42 on 2009-12-01
> **Roasted said:**
> Not to be a smart guy about that, but do they REALLY think it's a good idea to activate the overlay ALL of the time for any little thing you do on the system?

As far as I can tell, yes.
Read up on the [design document](http://www.gnome.org/~mccann/shell/design/GNOME_Shell-20091114.pdf)

My major gripe of course is not being able to see what's running and/or switch easily.

Their answer? Overlay or Alt-Tab.
Breadcrumbs are not part of the GNOME Shell, so they won't be in by default. It also conflicts with the actions GNOME wants when you click the application's name next to Activities.

They spent a lot of time on the overlay so they want it to be what you use for EVERYTHING.  A one stop shop for anything you want to do. Of course that sounds good on paper, but isn't too good in practice.

---

### Post by Roasted on 2009-12-01
> **Merk42 said:**
> As far as I can tell, yes.
Read up on the [design document](http://www.gnome.org/~mccann/shell/design/GNOME_Shell-20091114.pdf)

My major gripe of course is not being able to see what's running and/or switch easily.

Their answer? Overlay or Alt-Tab.
Breadcrumbs are not part of the GNOME Shell, so they won't be in by default. It also conflicts with the actions GNOME wants when you click the application's name next to Activities.

They spent a lot of time on the overlay so they want it to be what you use for EVERYTHING.  A one stop shop for anything you want to do. Of course that sounds good on paper, but isn't too good in practice.

It's not good in practice. At all. For about a minute I was like wowwww neato I really like this. But then I  started to feel... funny. Getting dizzy while multitasking isn't the way to go.

I don't mean to throw comparative sides into the mixture, but I think KDE has the right idea. You have your menu for opening applications and your task menu for minimizing/maximizing windows already open. Then, if you want the overlay, just drag the mouse to whatever corner of the screen you want. That way I know when I go to the menu, I'm hitting the menu. When I drag my mouse to the bottom of the screen (my panel is at the top) BAM - Overlay.

I really think Gnome Shell needs to adopt something of that nature to be as successful as the previous version is.

This is not really an argument for the way things are done in gnome-shell at the moment, just a general design issue.

### Post by RAOF on 2009-12-01
> **Merk42 said:**
> Your example is a little slanted as most of those applications you don't directly interact with.  They'd never show up in a menu list/alt-tab/etc

Right.  But why do I need a list of *any* of them?  What benefit do I get out of having a list of some subset of those processes, and what subset should be chosen?

> **Merk42 said:**
> 
More to the point though, do you think the fact that you cannot switch to an application without zoooming out to an overlay or requiring Alt-Tab to be a design issue?

No, not particularly.  I don't use a task list as it stands, and I don't miss it.  For people who might miss it, large thumbnails of their windows present significantly more useful information than tiny icons in the bottom panel.

**Edit**: I know this will require a slightly different work flow to what you currently use.  What I'm after is why the current workflow is better than the proposed workflow.

### Post by Reiger on 2009-12-02
> **RAOF said:**
> Why do you want to know what applications are running?  Why should I care whether firefox is running or not?  What does that buy me?

Because the world is not perfect and applications do not always anticipate your needs. A typical example is revolves around the notion of &#8216;session&#8217;: if you want to have a session with some application that is about -say- your website/portfolio in some editor you want to ensure that if you open index.html it is not a new session but rather part of another one.

And quite a few applications *do* have the idea that people open a session only once and then only use the application itself to modify the session state -- the notion of a concerted effort between file manager and application only exists on the meta-level of &#8216;integration&#8217; but certainly not as a modus of (application) execution: in other words the File Manager is not smart/integrated enough to offer &#8216;open in currently running instance&#8217; equivalents.  

> There's already a huge raft of binaries running that you don't know about.

True. But from a users point of view these binaries exist to make their applications possible and are therefore an implementation necessity and *not* something *you could possibly be bothered to care about*. From the users point of view applications and their settings are the show and the rest exists to keep it going on.

Essentially the difference is of the same nature when you go to a festival and want to know about the various acts that will be on stage but can't really be bothered about the schedule of stage crews.

### Post by RAOF on 2009-12-02
> **Reiger said:**
> Because the world is not perfect and applications do not always anticipate your needs. A typical example is revolves around the notion of ‘session’: if you want to have a session with some application that is about -say- your website/portfolio in some editor you want to ensure that if you open index.html it is not a new session but rather part of another one.

Actually, I think the overlay makes this use-case *easier*.  Since the way to start Firefox is from the overlay, you'll have a big firefox preview if it's already running.  So, at the point when you want to make the decision "do I want to start a new firefox, or switch to an existing instance" you've got good information to base this decision on.


> **Reiger said:**
> 
And quite a few applications *do* have the idea that people open a session only once and then only use the application itself to modify the session state -- the notion of a concerted effort between file manager and application only exists on the meta-level of ‘integration’ but certainly not as a modus of (application) execution: in other words the File Manager is not smart/integrated enough to offer ‘open in currently running instance’ equivalents.  

These applications should be single-instance.  It's not hard to do; there's a simple library to make it work.


> **click4851 said:**
> In your opinion.....In my world all knowledge is power, I decide what is relevant and what is not. Really thats up to the user, which means its going to be different depending on the use of the computer. If you try and second guess the user, aren't your headed down the path of the lowest common denominator solution?

So, would you like to have a list of running processes on screen all the time?  Do you think this should be the default?

Design *invariably* involves deciding what's relevant and what's not relevant.  This is why we don't have a list of running processes displayed on the screen by default; why firefox doesn't have a running list of HTTP headers sent/received in the status-bar; why pidgin doesn't show the raw xml of your XMPP conversations.  These are all situations where more information is *less* power - it's (for almost all users) irrelevant information that *obscures* the information that they need.

> **click4851 said:**
> 
Now that data <snip> is all running processes, I was under the impression we were referring to just applications running in the task bar? From Merk42 statement he seems to value the ability to see whats running and the ability to switch between applications, he even suggests his future 2 options Overlay vs Alt+Tab. Am I way off base?
I'm questioning *why* he values the ability to see what's running.  Or, rather, why he values having a list of running applications continuously visible - it's still possible to see what's running, and to switch between applications.  The difference is that the list of running applications is no longer on the screen all the time - in gnome-shell, it's only there when you want to switch (or start) applications.

What is insufficient about this?  What does a continuously visible list of running applications get you?

---

### Post by aethralis on 2009-12-02
I could probably find some study that confirms that if too many elements change under your view you need to process more information and this makes the task you are about to do more energy consuming. When I have an overview of my open applications just a glance below away (as it is now) the workspace stays mostly the same and I can plan ahead with minimal change of attention. 

As I'm working mostly with OOo and have sometimes five or more instances of different texts open, I have even very limited use of different docks (I have tried them and found unusable for such kind of work). Also the gnome shell way (tried that too) is for such purposes unusable. I would go not so far as to say that this is a wasted effort, but I would probably find that GS makes my work less productive. I tried to adapt to its concepts and in some respect its interesting and innovative, but the direction it is going now seems not to take into account such kind of work as I'm doing. If you have 6 text documents open they look quite similar in such expose view (overlay view?). And the current implementation of Alt+Tab does not help here either, as the documents get hidden behind screen borders and you have to no way to switch to them. All in all I'm really curious how it is planned to resolve such questions.

Alt-Tab switches between applications
Alt-` switches between documents of active application

### Post by 23meg on 2009-12-02
Meanwhile, Colin Walters is [about to land his extension system patch]("http://cgwalters.livejournal.com/26125.html"), which, it seems, will let you [monkey patch]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monkey_patch") the shell on the fly by loading arbitrary JS and CSS.

Which, in the context of recent posts to this thread, translates to: it seems that if people disagree with the way GNOME Shell handles task switching by default, the barriers in front of having their own way instead will be exceptionally low.

### Post by LiquidMeson on 2009-12-02
I like where this ([http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell/DesignerPlayground/BreadcrumbsEtc](http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell/DesignerPlayground/BreadcrumbsEtc)) is heading.

+color is defined by background |unless themed
-+most recent have richer color
--+alternate color if attention is needed

+maximised window merges into the top shell bar
(think ff4.0) applications should be able to modify its presence in the bar, alerts/ loading etc.

+all current workspace apps should be shown
-+non active apps that are running are compressed to icon form
--+there should be the ability to dock apps
ex. activities > workspace 1> (omgubuntu - firefox) (t) (r) (n)
note:while icon's may ruin the sheik of the top bar they could possibly be made white/black/transparent.
using icons to switch between apps is familiar, quick, and easy. Not to mention both osx and windows now use this as a primary form. As gnome begins to tie back into the web the icons could also represent website or even status markers.

(don't really need to know I'm in workspace 1, that's what activities is for ;D)

[https://wiki.mozilla.org/images/f/f6/Fx-4.0-Direction-Phase-01.png](https://wiki.mozilla.org/images/f/f6/Fx-4.0-Direction-Phase-01.png)
just thinking about ff4.0 makes me wonder...interesting if the interface was a modified version of that theme @_@
ex.
-home icon = activities
-hitting the +icon turned into a gnome-do search box. 
-minimize/close are merged back into current app like in the breadcrumbs images

Can't wait till 10/10/10. :popcorn: props to all the devs workin on this radical shift.

### Post by RAOF on 2009-12-03
> **Starks said:**
> RAOF, you're not doing a very good job of convincing us that gnome-shell is a good idea when you consider every flaw we point out to be a "feature".

gnome-shell is deliberately quite different.  I'm trying to work out (and help others work out) if the flaws are "it's different to what I'm used to" or "there's something that I need to do that it does worse".  We're all GNOME 2.0 power-users here - because gnome-shell has a quite different design I think we'll tend to think it's worse.  In a similar way that Windows power-users find the transition to GNOME harder - we're very, *very* used to the way things work now, and when the shell doesn't support the same workflow as easily, we tend to consider it a flaw.

I certainly don't think gnome-shell is flawless; for example, from an implementation perspective I don't think having the shell as an in-process plugin for the desktop compositor is a good idea.  I think some of the design ideas are good, and some are interesting, though.

---

my workarounds for this are currently

- gnome-do – not having to leave the keyboard is just great when working with texts

### Post by qamelian on 2009-12-03

### Post by Roasted on 2009-12-03
> **qamelian said:**
> I guess it depends on your point of view. I agree with RAOF on the vast majority of what he is saying, and in fact these are among the same reasons I prefer Gnome-shell to Gnome 2.X. I prefer it strongly enough that it has been my default desktop environment for a while now and I'm uncomfortable when I need to go back to "old" Gnome, as it now feels confining to me. When I first made the switch, I did need to make some minor adjustments to a couple of my work habits but that ended up being a good thing, as I find Gnome-shell a much more productive environment than the current Gnome.

As somebody who works in a large business environment, I honestly cannot see how Gnome Shell will take off in the business world for those people out there who utilize Ubuntu in their workplace currently. My boss was really looking into Ubuntu, but when he realized Gnome Shell was the future with no chance of using the standard panel setup, he was kind of bummed.... until I showed him Kubuntu. ;) The desktop environment that has the right idea between activation of the overlay while not forcing you to get dizzy every time you need to open up the menu. Genius.

I really cannot fathom that ridiculous overlay. I mean, it's great. But why activate it for EVERYTHING you do? It doesn't make sense... But like somebody else here intelligently said - To get out of your car to change your radio station is ridiculous.

If they don't make some simple changes to make the overlay activate a little more intelligently, I have severe doubt of Gnome Shell being the top dog it currently is, and even doubt in Gnome being the "default" desktop for Ubuntu in the future. I've used Gnome Shell extensively, and I am a huge Gnome fanatic who's used it exclusively for years. But I just don't know what those developers are thinking. It's a great idea, but something so simple such as the overlay being required for -everything- as well as no Compiz support (which fuels the Linux newcomers) just doesn't make much sense. Not only that, but the panels SHOULD absolutely positively be an option. Period. Done. Over. It should be an option. I know what it's like when you try to integrate a new application, newer operating system, newer computer, or anything like that to standard users. But geez, I'd get hung if I ever dropped something that got a severe overhaul in front of somebody and said, sorry, this is what you got. Enjoy.

*sigh*

### Post by ibuclaw on 2009-12-03
> **RAOF said:**
> gnome-shell is deliberately quite different.  I'm trying to work out (and help others work out) if the flaws are "it's different to what I'm used to" or "there's something that I need to do that it does worse".  We're all GNOME 2.0 power-users here - because gnome-shell has a quite different design I think we'll tend to think it's worse.  In a similar way that Windows power-users find the transition to GNOME harder - we're very, *very* used to the way things work now, and when the shell doesn't support the same workflow as easily, we tend to consider it a flaw.

I certainly don't think gnome-shell is flawless; for example, from an implementation perspective I don't think having the shell as an in-process plugin for the desktop compositor is a good idea.  I think some of the design ideas are good, and some are interesting, though.
What we also have to bare in mind too is that we aren't going to get a taste of Gnome Shell in Ubuntu for another 11 months. To put it in the more comical/nostalgic way - that is nearly 2 years in Linux time. ;)

So the point is - don't make final judgments on something that will likely change radically in the meantime.

The move from Metacity -> Mutter is imminent.
The move from Gnome-Panel -> ??? is still technically not defined yet. But then again - there appears to be no one else who is defining such a change, so one can assume people are generally happy with Gnome-Shell filling in that gap.
> **Starks said:**
> RAOF, you're not doing a very good job of convincing us that gnome-shell is a good idea when you consider every flaw we point out to be a "feature".
In case you weren't aware of the distinction: [http://www.globalnerdy.com/2007/12/04/bug-vs-feature/](http://www.globalnerdy.com/2007/12/04/bug-vs-feature/)

### Post by qamelian on 2009-12-03
> **Roasted said:**
> As somebody who works in a large business environment, 
I also work in a large business environment, as well as supporting several business clients that I have migrated to Ubuntu Linux. The user testing I've done with those clients using Gnome-shell has almost uniformly been favourable, so I don't really share your bleak outlook. Most of the users I support who have been involved in the testing found it very easy to adapt to Gnome-shell and a sizeable majority have also expressed a preference for the principles behind Gnome-shell as well. It still needs work, but in the environment I support, it is already proving to be quite usable by most of my end-users.

### Post by bash on 2009-12-03
On a gaming related note:

When I was using gnome-shell the other day I wanted to see how gaming is currently under gnome-shell. Mostly to see how the performance would be or if it would just plain crash. So I started up a game in WINE. It worked fine, went as expected to fullscreen mode and even the performance was decent. So far, so boring.

Then I remembered something that has always bugged me about gaming under Linux in fullscreen mode, there nothing easy like Alt-Tab in Windows to minimize the game. So I got an idea and wondered if maybe the overlay mode would still work. And after pressing Super key, to my surprise it actually went to the overview mode. 

It works smoothly. And if you move the game to it's own workspace, you can happily access other programms on the other workspaces and then switch back to the game. I tried it out a couple of times and never did it crash the shell or the game. 

If intentional or not, this feature already makes the shell for my gaming related needs way more usefull than the current setup. I actually find this solution even more seamless and fluid than Alt-Tab in Windows.

### Post by VeeDubb on 2009-12-04
For those who don't like 'needing' to go to the overlay, there is a REALLY great compromise that I would suggest.  Bare in mind, I've only been using gnome-shell part time, and even then, I've only used it for a couple of days.

Install cairo-dock.

It kicks the snot out of AWN and every other bar, it can be made to auto hide, and will allow you to maintain your current "Start Menu" until you either get over the need for one (which I haven't) or until gnome-shell is smoothed out a little.


As it sits, gnome-shell is actually really quite complete, IMHO, but just very very different.  Adding a cairo-dock to it, in auto-hide mode with just the stuff you absolutely need, really smooths out some of the rough edges.

> For those who don't like 'needing' to go to the overlay, there is a REALLY great compromise that I would suggest.  Bare in mind, I've only been using gnome-shell part time, and even then, I've only used it for a couple of days.

Install cairo-dock.

It kicks the snot out of AWN and every other bar, it can be made to auto hide, and will allow you to maintain your current "Start Menu" until you either get over the need for one (which I haven't) or until gnome-shell is smoothed out a little.


As it sits, gnome-shell is actually really quite complete, IMHO, but just very very different.  Adding a cairo-dock to it, in auto-hide mode with just the stuff you absolutely need, really smooths out some of the rough edges.

The only problem with Cairo Dock is it pretty much acts like it's in Alpha stage. I installed Cairo Dock on 3 different computers and had 3 different sets of problems. It may work nicely for you, but they have a long way to go to be really stable. The only dock I would recommend to anybody without thinking twice is Gnome-Do's Docky, since it's just simple and works nicely.

After using KDE for a while now, I'm liking the single panel setup they got going on, so I really don't see the need for a dock (for me) anymore.

I don't know, like I've said a few times, I plan to stick with KDE now that I've been exposed to it more, but I still want to see Gnome Shell succeed, and I feel like a few simple things can be done to make the entire interface that much better, but they seem to think the exact opposite. *shrug*

---

### Post by gspat on 2009-12-19
Hmm.. seems more deconstructive than constructive...

What most everyone seems to want is a method to switch to open applications without relying on keyboard shortcuts, or watching the screen flip around and re-orient themselves while waiting to produce the required mouse click. (my opinion)

Everyone seems to forget that a vast majority of new users (re: Bug # 1) will not use the keyboard for anything besides writing emails, IMs, letters or typing an address in the browser. Most windows users I help when clearing out malware, etc. don't even seem to know about ctrl-alt-del to get to the task manager.

A close friend who looks into trends has told me that touch will be the next big thing really taking off in about 6 - 12 months... read that as 17 - 19 inch screen with no keyboard connected. How do they plan to use alt-tab for that? Or, as per usual, do they just plan to ignore what comes and do their own thing without regard, then play catch up again later.

I've been working towards learning javascript so I can write some stuff to pass on, but between the learning curve and time constraints, it will probably be too late by the time I learn it. I've passed an idea and an issue I've had on to the mailing list, but they will probably be ignored.

---

### Post by Merk42 on 2009-12-19
> **gspat said:**
> A close friend who looks into trends has told me that touch will be the next big thing really taking off in about 6 - 12 months... read that as 17 - 19 inch screen with no keyboard connected. How do they plan to use alt-tab for that? Or, as per usual, do they just plan to ignore what comes and do their own thing without regard, then play catch up again later.

Touch in that manner has been shown to have more problems than benefits.
The two main ones being that your finger/hand/arm is blocking your view and holding your arms out like that for extending periods of time causes fatigue.

There was a sort of take on it with either multitouch in mice or a large tablet, but not on the screen.

Also in your point there would still be a keyboard wouldn't there? Even if you touched directly onto the screen, how would you do things like chatting and word processing with no keyboard?
On screen keyboard? Aside from that being an awful idea, it would still provide "Alt-Tab"

---

### Post by gspat on 2009-12-19
If I understand you correctly, by fatiguing, you  mean having to reach for the display (as in being in a more traditional location, like at the back of your desk or mounted on a wall).

The display he talks about for consumer level use are larger than standard handheld devices and are approx. the size of an opened up magazine or if larger, an opened newspaper that lays directly on your desk, coffe table etc and has no reason to be more fatiging than  a standard keyboard as the display height would be no higher than a standard keyboard. A good analogy as I type this is to take my 22" LCD display and lay it flat on my computer desk in the spot I have my keyboard in right now.

Your hands do not get in the way if you subscribe to certain main design aspects:

1 - main controls for use of O/S are unobtrusive and placed along the LRS, RHS or bottom of the display area.

This is where gnome-shell immediately reminded me of what he was talking about with the overlay along the edge.

This makes for a much less fatiguing method of item/application selection as your hands are mostly at rest and for common use your index finger or thumb are used for initial selections. this is meant to make the initial selections as unobtrusive on the display as possible.

2 - Selections that result in other menu options should intelligently display themselves dependant on where they are on screen.

This is where I personally think the alt-tab approach fails, in that it should not be at the centre of the screen, but rather in the bottom corner and the arrow denoting expansion required to display multiple application windows should be pointing up and the resulting thumbnails be above the initial displayed items.

3 - Non changing or infrequently used items placed to the left or right of the top of the display.

I think at present the shell has the right idea on this as well

3 - On Screen keyboard always displays at the bottom centre of the screen.

4 - The upper centre of the display should be mainly used for informational display purposes only.

Now, having said this, it may seem untenable, but my friend has seen test units making use of this stuff. and he claims it is very easy to use.

The current thought among the designers is that the application gui designer will have to think out how to best make their application usable (from a GUI standpoint) on these devices, but that accepted norms for them will come, probably using design aspects from the O/S itself.

---

### Post by macroshaft on 2009-12-19
I have to say it, for such a great idea this really sucks!

I love the way everyone has been so passionate about it so I thought I'll give it a go and see what happens. Firstly it won't install on Lucid from the repositories, but I understand this has had dependency problems as is to be expected in alpha testing. My desktop on Karmic installs but runs like a snail, the overlay works and the taskbar is there but everything else is plain black so the system is unusable. The icons in the top right are garbled.

Luckily my laptop with Karmic works a treat. I'm having a fair degree of culture shock, I miss my lower taskbar. However there are a few points I feel aren't going to go away, when accessing the overlay I noticed that the preferances and system settings are not present. If I use the find box then the utilities in these menus appear but I can't casually browse them. I suddenly realised I can't remember what is in the menu without looking at it and I was stuck. Also I'm instinctively using Alt+Tab and find that now it is no longer possible to switch between 2 of the open windows, say browser and chat window, because Alt+Tab blindly switches through the list, ignoring what you last had on screen.

One big show stopper for me is that it has a nasty habit of ignoring my mouse clicks in the taskbar and overlay, it took 10 attempts, clicking everywhere, using the windows button to close then open the overlay etc just to open this browser.

All that said it's looking great and I think given all the months left until this could possibly become the default it's bound to polish up nicely.

Can anyone tell me if the black screen thing is a known issue or should I report this?

---

A good analogy as I type this is to take my 22" LCD display and lay it flat on my computer desk in the spot I have my keyboard in right now.

Your hands do not get in the way if you subscribe to certain main design aspects:

1 - main controls for use of O/S are unobtrusive and placed along the LRS, RHS or bottom of the display area.

This is where gnome-shell immediately reminded me of what he was talking about with the overlay along the edge.

This makes for a much less fatiguing method of item/application selection as your hands are mostly at rest and for common use your index finger or thumb are used for initial selections. this is meant to make the initial selections as unobtrusive on the display as possible.

2 - Selections that result in other menu options should intelligently display themselves dependant on where they are on screen.

This is where I personally think the alt-tab approach fails, in that it should not be at the centre of the screen, but rather in the bottom corner and the arrow denoting expansion required to display multiple application windows should be pointing up and the resulting thumbnails be above the initial displayed items.

3 - Non changing or infrequently used items placed to the left or right of the top of the display.

3 - On Screen keyboard always displays at the bottom centre of the screen.

As much as this sounds nice, I can assure you a secretary that has to be typing most of the day will certainly suffer the consequences. People constantly typing need a firm suport for their wrists, or the consequences can be very negative.

Also, a computer laying flat in a table is awful for your neck. This may not seem very relevant at first, but after years of work, it can derive into invalidating conditions...If you put it in an angle, then your wrists will suffer.

In the end, I think the keyboard is an awesome tool, that is often looked upon.

### Post by ronacc on 2009-12-19
I can attest to what orlox is saying . Touch screens sound like such a neat and "gee whiz" idea but I do believe that they will prove to be impractical for day to day use . I just the other day had a chance to try out a desktop running windows 7 with a touch screen and very soon found myself reaching for the mouse . With the display upright as a normal monitor it is horribly positioned for using an on screen keyboard and laying flat is a poor position for prolonged reading even without the obstruction of the hands poised over said onscreen keyboard . and I believe that selecting a single line of type or a few words to highlight would be difficult if not impossible at any reasonable font size , a fingertip would cover more than one line or letter . Also since selecting requires 2 gestures it would require 2 hands , 2 fingers on one hand would not do .Touch screens may very well become the favored paradigm for certain niche applications , the Iphone comes to mind, but I do not think they will become the mainstream way of doing things .

### Post by laborg on 2009-12-28
I'd love to see the functionality of Gnome-do (not docky) incorporated by the GS. The first thing I would do is to write a plugin which lets me specify where or what to search by prefixing a single character (opera has that if i remember correctly).
eg.:
"l math.txt" -> search for "math.txt" in local files
"g jean rollin" -> search for "jean rollin" in google
"w cucumber" -> search for "cucumber" in wikipedia
"firefox" -> default to applications (and if not found, local files)

I can't imagine a way of being faster than that...

best regards,

### Post by Reiger on 2010-01-02
Gnome Shell is not for Lucid. The simple fact that default Lucid repositories will not let you install gnome-shell because the dependencies have not been updated properly (gobject-introspect -> gir rename) testifies to that.

At best it is for Lucid +1 since Gnome Shell is not to be default in Gnome itself until the ~ September 2010 release at the earliest.

### Post by madmax75 on 2010-01-06

First time: didn't work. At all.

Second time: worked for two whole seconds (yay!), then froze my desktop. I finally managed to come up with a terminal window and shut down the computer.

Just another layer of completely useless eye-candy that gets in your way. Sure, it might look "cool" and "neat" and "radical" much like Compiz effects - which also are mostly completely useless waste of resources and just get in your way all the time -  and you can impress the Medieval village folks with it for five minutes, but that's pretty much it.

When and if this becomes the standard for Gnome desktop, it's time to switch to something more useful.

The whole gnome-shell thing just has a "catastrophic error" written all over it, from the user perspective. At least at this point. Maybe the future releases are better and more functional, but I still have a bad feeling about this. I like my desktop to be simple, swift and efficient for me. I don't need "cool" but useless effects to slow everything down.

### Post by qamelian on 2010-01-06
> **Schendje said:**
> I know you've been using it for a while (and also that you've been testing it with other people), so I wanted to ask you: how do you feel about switching applications? Do you use a dock, or Alt+Tab, or the top left corner?

That's the biggest annoyance for me. Do you recommend using it for a while to get used to it? :)

I never use a dock. Although I try each of the popular docks from time to time, I find them more of a hindrance than anything else. I use Alt-Tab to switch between apps, much the same way I did on the "old" Gnome desktop. If I have many applications open across a number of workspaces, I use the keyboard shortcut to invoke the Activities menu / overlay. I seldom use the mouse unless I have very specific need of it, as most apps I use on a regular basis as just as easy or easier to operate using the keyboard and manipulating the mouse only slows me down.

And yes, it did take me a little time to get used to it and begin to like it. I did need to adjust some of my work habits when I started using Gnome-shell, but now I realize that the habits I had to change were bad habits anyway. I find the Gnome-shell environment to be much more productive for me.

### Post by SKLP on 2010-01-07
I've now been exclusively using gnome-shell on both my laptops (a 17" with nvidia graphics , and a 10" intel gma netbook). (FRom the ricotz ppa)

I like the ability to launch apps in a manner similar to gnome-do by pressing Super and typing, I also like that the same button provides exposé-like functionality. To me, it overall seems more ready for use than compiz ever was.
Also, it finally removes one of the (IMO) worst things about the windowsXP/linux desktop, the taskbar.

With gnome-shell I mostly don't use multiple desktops (just sometimes) and I basically do not use minimize. The exposé functionality works great, imo.

Some notable flaws: 
* The panel at the top can get a bit empty, maybe they should have some kind of applets to add to all the empty space in the middle? (e.g. CPU usage and so on). Another idea would be to switch to a mac-style menu bar for GNOME3  and put it there (between Activities and a right-aligned clock).
* The search feature is not as good as gnome-do

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-07
> **qamelian said:**
> I'm sorry but if you really believe this, then I have a hard time believing you tried it at all. I can't think of a single piece of "eye candy" in Gnome-shell that doesn't in some way enhance usability for me.

While I do believe the vast majority of the compiz effects are strictly for eye candy, the whole realm of compiz in itself does enhance the desktop usability experience, and a good number of compiz features do indeed make for better efficiency on the desktop if you use them right.

> If it doesn't work for you, then by all means, you are free to change to an environment that works better for you. I can't think of one right now that works better for me than Gnome-shell. To each his own.


That's the beauty of Linux, and something I am very thankful we have. I have used Gnome-Shell extensively. I even showed a couple of my hardcore Linux-loving friends it. All the while, I kept my mouth shut because I didn't want to influence any biased opinions of myself on them. Each of them were very "meh" over it. I got good reactions with it right away because oh yeah the overlay is neato and look at the workspaces yeah that's great. But the overall practicality level of Gnome-Shell in an actual business environment isn't really adding up, to be completely blunt.

Last year, if my boss came to me and said hey look let's deploy a Linux lab at one of the schools, I would have probably chosen Ubuntu. After seeing Gnome-Shell and realizing this is the future of Gnome, I would have went for KDE. Being that Kubuntu isn't "all there" with KDE (in my opinion when compared to other KDE distros), my current answer would be on deploying either openSuSE or Mandriva 2010.

I just feel like Gnome-Shell is too focused on being simple that they actually took away some practical levels that the Gnome desktop was popular for. Do I really have to activate the overlay every time I want to open a file, folder, or application? Does that button to expand the menu for "more" applications really have to be that small? In fact, I even signed up to the mailing list and contacted the developers about it, stating that I believe the "more" button should be enlarged to enhance usability. Their answer? They actually decided to make it smaller. *sigh* 

Gnome-Shell has given me the opportunity, reason, and justification to look for something else to better suit my needs. I have found KDE to be exceptional with its stability and ease of use they have deployed. They've come a long way from the 4.0 release, and it's worth noting.


> Believe what you like. I've done usability studies with my clients that say you are wrong.

Like you said, to each his own. There's nothing wrong whatsoever about somebody liking or disliking Gnome-Shell. Every desktop environment will have its ups and downs, pros and cons, etc. 

At the end of the day, none of this really matters. We're all using Linux. Whether it's a debian distro or rpm distro or kde or lxde or gnome, it just doesn't matter. I.E. - we're all in a bar. You might like guinness. I may hate it. But you're drinking what you're drinking, I'm drinking what I'm drinking, and we're all having a good time. :popcorn:

---

### Post by BwackNinja on 2010-01-07
I think a lot of the reason why people don't like gnome-shell right now is that it isn't exactly how they want to use it right now. Until it reaches that level of maturity where people can really customize it to be the system that they want, it won't reach all the audiences it probably will eventually get. Gnome-shell changes the game irreversibly and doesn't give you a chance to go halfway back, because its a shell AND a window manager.

Compiz got it right with its plugins, each of which being separate, individually usable, and completely configurable without going into too much trouble. It doesn't really force anything on you at all. You can't really hate compiz, you can hate its defaults, you can hate individual plugins, but as a base, anything you can really hate is probably a bug.

Its an all or nothing game with gnome-shell. You can't only like parts of it -- its the whole thing or nothing. I, for example, like the overlay and how it keeps my most used apps most accessible and how it lets me see my other workspaces easily at the same time. What I don't like about that system is that it adds extra animations when I might just want to open an application. It goes so out of its way not to obscure your desktop, while moving and scaling your windows to all be seen. I don't really want that.

Gnome-shell's overlay is a one-stop shop for everything you can really do with it. That might be putting too much functionality in one place -- I might want a specialty store somewhere. They're talking about making nautilus for example do less and be just a file manager for gnome 3. KDE4 looks nicer (because of all qt4 and good theme engines), does more, but it doesn't really change the game. Gnome3 is more about changing the game. At most, I heard of people not liking KDE4 because of instability, and because of some apps changing and not having everything that they used to, like Amarok2. The rest of Gnome3 stuff, like deprecating a bunch of things and moving things away, breaking compatibility: that's stuff I can completely agree with. Gnome-shell is another thing, and I refuse to make any complete judgment of it until its officially released, maybe even waiting for the second official release.

---

### Post by LiquidMeson on 2010-01-07
"At the end of the day, none of this really matters. We're all using Linux."

While this may be true for those on the forum, I would really like to see Gnu/Linux reach a much larger crowd, as in becoming the majority. Ubuntu I feel has a very strong current going for it, but if the discussions going on in the forums are any proof I feel this could put a damper on things.

My main Pc (Amd) dual boots 7 and Ubuntu, while my laptop is typically Ubuntu Dev/stable (Intel hardware). I love Linux for its core and Ubuntu for its stability and ease of use, but I still very much prefer the w7 interface.

If shell is to move forward it needs to feel clean and light (software wise this is true, but not in appearance). w7 has done a great job of this. The operating system is becoming less about the features and more about the artwork as all that becomes really necessary is the Internet. Gnome-shell cannot ignore the interfaces of Firefox or Chrome as these are the interfaces the new generation is growing up using. If that includes a dock, then so be-it. In general people would rather not use computers unless for entertainment, and the fact that gnome-shell has a clock in the middle of the task bar is a sign of mixed priorities.

Shell may be better at keeping the user organized, but people don't switch computers or buy a new one so they can be more organized. Typically.

This is also becoming true for businesses as well, as things become more decentralized with the help and ease of communication methods. Communication! should be key followed by ease of use, customization, and beauty. Just look at Google it is clean and very simple (like gnome-do) yet allows for a multitude of things. It doesn't tell you how to organize, it just makes it easier to get things done. Of course this is not to say the shell concept is a bad idea, just that gnome's focus is in the wrong direction.

Business is what made Microsoft popular
Beauty, Apple
Communication, Linux.

If people wanted efficiency they'd buy a solar powered digital watch like my'n with a stop watch and all the extra stuff, but most have classy slick watches or maybe one with Micky mouse on it. Most kids (here in silicon valley anyway) don't even have watches, just cell phones. They need to know the time so they can get to school, but once school is out decisions and activities are carried out by calling someone or text-ing them, mostly with only a relative discrepancy for time. It isn't organization that matters so much as communication and personality.

---
