### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-08
> **qamelian said:**
> It's all really subjective. Personally, I can't stand KDE 4.3, in part because it literally crawls on the same laptop on which Gnome-shell flies, and in part because I find it incredibly clumsy and cumbersome to use compared to Gnome-shell. KDE always feel like its in my face whereas Gnome-shell always feel like it out of my way until I need it. In my opinion, the last genuinely usable version of KDE was 2.2. I haven't liked a single release since.

I have yet to come across a machine there KDE 4.3 slugs along where Gnome-Shell flies. Each instance has always been relatively comparable, unless you have an old machine and you're running compositing effects which would no doubt slug it along. On my machine, directly comparing Ubuntu and Kubuntu on their separate native installs, KDE uses about 30mb less than Gnome does on fresh boot.

But at the same token, who really cares about 30mb when people are pumping 4 8 16 gb of ram into their systems now-a-days. But even still, it's worth noting that KDE is not the fat pig Gnome enthusiasts put it out to be.

I cannot tell you how pissed off I was when I saw what Gnome was doing with Shell. I knew I had to find another desktop interface. But what? Years of hearing Gnome bash KDE made me truly feel as though KDE sucked when I hadn't extensively used it. What else? XFCE? Meh. LXDE? Meh. MAN am I glad I tried KDE. Which brings me to my next point...

> **benjamimgois said:**
> +1 , i agree with you completely. But i still think that KDE4 is doing a much better job, and at least for me, so much ahead gnome shell and Zeit!@##@...

This. Quoted for truth.


I'm sure once Gnome-Shell matures, I'll use it as well. But Gnome users, riddle me this. Hardcore Gnome users gave KDE a ton of crap when they changed over code to migrate from KDE 3.5 to KDE 4.0. Granted, 4.0 had problems, as anything else would with a new batch of code. 4.1 solved a ton of them. 4.2 a ton more. Beginning with 4.2 it was pretty damn stable. What do you guys think will happen to Gnome-Shell? They're revamping code from ground up. Just like KDE did...

It's just some food for thought. But just keep in mind, I'm not your KDE fan-boy that you often see. I've used Gnome for about 5 years, and KDE for only about 2 months. I just like to play devil's advocate so users might see the flip side of things. :P

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-08

Same for me. That's why I said:

> **Roasted said:**

I'm sorry you had the luck you did with KDE, although I'm not entirely convinced that you really utilized KDE in any productive manner to hold any true bearing against it in regard to its performance. You say it's always bad, it's always slow, you haven't had a single good experience with it, etc. I have used LXDE, XFCE, Gnome, Gnome Shell, KDE 3.5, KDE 4.2+, and ALL of them were relatively snappy to use without any sort of indication between them that they were lagging. The ONLY time I had issues with KDE was when I had compositing enabled and I was using a crappy graphics card. Try Gnome with Compiz enabled and you'll get the same results.

Point being, I have used all of the common desktop environments, and while some of them piss me off more than anything (i.e. shell), I still give it credit where it deserves it in regard to its performance. My negative opinion towards Shell was from a personal preference and usability stance, not a performance stance. I can't lie - I have to give these products credit where they deserve it instead of just holding a grudge against it.

I encourage you to try KDE again with an open mindset.

### Post by shafin on 2010-01-11
I have been running gnome-shell on a spare account for about 6 months now and I like its ideas. But I am in no way ready to give up compiz for gnome-shell. Usability wise,  compiz has great advantages, which gnome-shell plain ignores. In my machine, compiz 'feels' fluent while gnome-shell feels heavy. And what gnome shell is providing can be easily be achieved with compiz expo+scale+gnome do with hot corners being set up.

In the long run, some interesting things might happen. Here is this [interesting blog post,]("http://blog.thesilentnumber.me/2009/09/dropping-compiz-for-mutter-metacity-3.html") the author, who is a developer suggested dropping compiz right now, and as you can see from the comments, had a quite negative reaction. I found the comment of Travis watkins particularly interesting: 
> Few points here, some already mentioned but I just want to roll them all up into one post:

1) Mutter compositing requires the same or higher 3D acceration when compared to compiz. Some of this may be a lack of optimizations but I have a feeling a lot of it is just due to the fact that they use a full scenegraph for their implementation (clutter).

2) Mutter will almost certainly never have as many effects as compiz or even as many as the default Ubuntu configuration for compiz. Metacity, even in the composited/gnome-shell age, is the Cheerios of Window Managers.

3) 10.04 LTS will almost certainly use the same 2.28 version of GNOME we have now with major a couple exceptions for things like empathy so long as they do not depend on any GTK+ 3/GNOME 3 stuff so we'll certainly have compiz in this release.

4) Compiz is actually more likely to kill metacity in Ubuntu the way things are going now. Metacity is becoming 3D only while compiz is gaining support for running without compositing and with 2D XRender compositing (like metacity does now). This work should be ready in time to 10.10 so unless we decide to fully embrace gnome-shell and the GNOME 3 way there I would expect metacity to be kicked off the CD before compiz.

I took my cue from it and yesterday replaced my top gnome panel with AWN 0.4, It has matured into an almost complete replacement. So far so good.

If gnome-shell fails to achieve feature parity with compiz, I'd rather have an ubuntu with compiz+AWN or any other panel replacement.

---

### Post by LiquidMeson on 2010-01-13
I have uploaded the current build which works for me.
meson.us/x/GnomeShell/ShellBuildJan12.tar.bz2

I recommend deleting what you currently have of gnome-shell. Extract the contents to your home directory and run the usual update commands in the terminal:

bash gnome-shell-build-setup.sh && bin/jhbuild

and finally to start it up

gnome-shell/source/gnome-shell/src/gnome-shell -r

This version was built on amd64 so it might have to recompile.
Not sure if this will work.., its 74.3MB total.:shock:
note:this was run in lucid, karmic will have old packages that might not work.

### Post by Ibidem on 2010-01-14
A look is enough for me...It *almost* looks like they borrowed the inspiration from M$ (Vista), with all that junk stuck on one menu.  Additionally, it's kind of a waste to have something like that running on the GPU.
I never really cared for GNOME, but this is definitely underwhelming.
I think a window manager shouldn't get in the way or pull too much by way of resources (CPU, GPU, RAM, or disk).
If it does, there'd better be something to show for it (Compiz, for instance).
That's why I use IceWM.

As far as specifics:
Why stick everything in one place?  In particular, the usage-based grouping is an inconvenience. 

Don't take this as flamebait; but I vote for not making this default any time soon.
Ibidem

I sure hope I am right, Lxde is fine but I prefer gnome.  I am not compatible with KDE.  Xfce is kind of silly in a lot of ways, at least in Xubuntu (I like Zenix).Well if they don't I'll resort to FluxBox and even that isn't what I would like. To much difficulty finding separate /home partitions etc where as Gnome knows it is there.

Don't get me wrong I like the look of Gnome Shell but it just wont work on my main machine as it stands now.

### Post by [h2o] on 2010-01-14
> **Ibidem said:**
> A look is enough for me...It *almost* looks like they borrowed the inspiration from M$ (Vista), with all that junk stuck on one menu.  Additionally, it's kind of a waste to have something like that running on the GPU.
I never really cared for GNOME, but this is definitely underwhelming.
I think a window manager shouldn't get in the way or pull too much by way of resources (CPU, GPU, RAM, or disk).
If it does, there'd better be something to show for it (Compiz, for instance).
That's why I use IceWM.
My desktop has a powerful CPU and GPU and lots of RAM. If I don't use those resources on a daily basis then what's the point of me buying them in the first place?
I do not mean that applications should be sloppily written or have unnecessary features and consume more resources than it needs.
The only problem would be if the DE is "stealing" resources while  using other applications. If I am gaming or doing something which requires a lot of computing resources I obviously do not wish to be handicapped by a hungry DE. That said, I have no fear that Gnome with Gnome Shell will be noticably slower than current Gnome.

Agreed.

Last year if my boss came to me and said hey look let's deploy a Linux lab, I'd choose Ubuntu, because I felt as though Ubuntu and Gnome were both solid.

If he asked me today let's deploy a Linux lab, I'd say Mandrivia, no doubt. Their implementation of KDE and their stability and ease of use, while still having a good bundle of high end features (much comparable to Ubuntu's ease of use vs advanced feature standpoint) is very solid.

So all in all, Gnome Shell pushed me to KDE, which further pushed me to other distros besides *buntu since I didn't feel as though Kubuntu was as exciting to use as other distros like Sidux, MEPIS, Mandriva, or openSUSE.

I hope Gnome Shell turns out to surprise everybody and be really solid. After all, it's still in development. I have severe doubts though. :( Either way I'll probably forever remain in KDE land. :)

### Post by qamelian on 2010-01-14
> **Roasted said:**
> Quite honestly the only reason I would think a dock would be ideal with Gnome Shell is because the top panel of Gnome Shell doesn't seem to allow shortcut icons. Something I used extensively in Ubuntu with Gnome 2.X. I also used Gnome 2.X with the bottom panel gone, replaced by Docky which auto hides. I gotta admit - that was really nice. The Docky developers are probably the most optimistic and helpful I've ever seen before. I was in an IRC with them and they literally begged me to use Docky much more in an attempt to try and break it, so I could report the bug to them so they could make it more robust.

What is the fully recommended way to go about installing Gnome Shell? I was just using the repo from 9.10 and rolling with it that way. Is there a higher recommended method? Preferably something that won't require 2 hours of hacking and troubleshooting to get rolling?
If I had to use a dock, I'm afraid I would have to choose AWN over Docky every time, just because the extensibility of AWN gives it a better shot at actually being useful to me. For my purposes though, Docky only succeeds in being a way to waste screen real estate that I have better uses for. I don't rely on shortcuts on the panel now, although I can remember a time when I'd have shortcuts for all my most used apps up there. :)

I've tried both the version from the repos and the build method provided on the gnome-shell page at gnome.org. Personally, I prefer to "roll my own", but I can't say there is any logical reason for that other than it allowed me to use current builds as opposed to the out-dated ones that appeared in the official repos. There is also the ricotz PPA which provides fairly up to date builds. If you want to test essentially the latest and greatest, but don't want to spend a half hour or so compiling it yourself, the ricotz PPA is probably the way to go.

### Post by aethralis on 2010-01-14
I'm using the g-s now and have done so for over a month, but I have to admit that for work purposes it does not cut it (yet). First of all too many crashes (as expected from alpha software), but it really does not help with the overall experience. Acrobat (I know I should be using evince, but for several reasons I still prefer acrobat) crashes g-s very often. This is admittedly no big surprise, but also OOo (specifically Impress) has problems. 

As for g-s, I don't mind the "recent items" (a bit crowded, but actually I nearly never use it), but I find the "places and devices" area unimaginative. Everything else is mostly ok and for some purposes even better than Gnome 2... but the application/task changing and overview of open applications is still uncomfortable for me. Alt+Tab is in itself wonderful, but if I have many windows of the same app open I have to hunt for the right window (for example I have 3 or more FF windows open then it takes an extra mouse movement to get there). Still I use mostly Alt+Tab because when copying pictures from FF to Impress (e.g.) the whole expose thing is a bit disorientating. Admittedly the current taskbar system is not ideal either.

I like the notifications of g-s better than the Ubuntu implementation. Notifications at the bottom of the screen are really less distracting than the upper right corner. 

Overall - I can imagine myself using it full time, but I would like to see some improvements in the area of task switching and file management.

---

### Post by OlyPerson on 2010-01-14
I've been using GS on Karmic for a month and a half or so now and the more I use it, the more I like it.  A couple dislikes though:
[LIST]
[*]The applet implementation on top doesn't allow you to put your own up yet, would be nice if it would.
[*]Why is the clock so prevelent?  I like it in G2 where it's just on the side, I generally don't care that it's Thu, why waste that space?
[*]Laggy, but it's still in alpha form so it's alright.
[*]the "Applications" menu displays an unsorted list of applications, the GS in the repos had it sorted, not sure why they switched to one long list.
[*]No dock.  I just use Docky, but I think they should include a dock with the final release.
[*]Has crashed a couple times with OOo, but otherwise very stable.
[*]Can't select anything in the upper bar when in the zoom out thingy.
[/LIST]

A few things I like:
[LIST]
[*]Love being able to press the super button, start typing in an application/place/file and having it there.  Just discovered this a couple weeks ago and now I think it's the best feature, I really dislike GS before then to be honest.
[*]It's pretty, good for showing off 'cool Linux stuff'
[*]Stable for the most part.
[/LIST]

My suggestions:
[LIST]
[*]Use the bar up top, right now nothing useful is up there, other than the time and network manager (although I just use the Docky add-on so really I never use the top bar ever)
[*]Be able to delete things from recent items list, some of use aren't  saints, I'll just leave it at that...
[*]Make "Places and Devices" more presentable.
[*]Sort applications in the "more applications" menu.
[*]Since there is no dock by default, include "Desktop" in the alt-tab selections like in Win7
[*]Last, and most needed before the final release, configurability.
[/LIST]

Just my two cents, not sure if anyone on here is a GS developer that could look into those, I think like my gripes wouldn't be hard to implement.

Does anyone know of a place to see what the GS team is working on?  I find it interesting to follow development, even though I'm not that technical of a user.

---

### Post by Psumi on 2010-01-14
OlyPerson,

you forgot that you can't drag applications from GS to a dock.

you forgot Ubuntu Software Center does not appear in GS Applications.

you forgot that ALT+Tab doesn't work, it uses Metacity's, which only works for the current workspace.

you forgot that workspaces cannot be configured by default in GS, even if you specify 4 in metacity, it doesn't show four in GS by default.

you forgot that there is a pattern to removing GS workspaces (You can't remove any non-used workspace you wish, you have to remove it in the pattern specified.

you forgot that GS requires composition, this is bad for us lower end computer users.

---

### Post by knarf on 2010-01-15
> **gspat said:**
> I really think bashing the compositing thing is overblown...

I set up compiz on my sister-in-laws PIII with a shitty 32MB graphics card and it worked fine for wobbly windows and the cube albiet slow...
Ah, but you're forgetting a whole class of machines which is currently perfectly able to run Gnome but will be pushed off the platform if Gnome Shell ever becomes standard. I'm talking about notebooks from the beginning of the 21st century, like the IBM ThinkPad T23 I'm using to enter this posting. This is my main machine, perfectly able to run most everything I throw at it with its 1.2 GHz PIII-m as long as it does not need fancy 3D graphics. The 'graphics processor' does not support non-power-of-two textures and as such can not be used with Compiz or Gnome Shell. Compositing in itself works fine with Metacity - albeit a bit slow - but Gnome Shell (or rather the graphics libraries it is based on) does not need just compositing but also non-power-of-two textures which are not supported by earlier GPU's.

I'd suggest changing GS to make compositing non-mandatory. It should be possible to overlay those fancy effects instead of compositing the whole bunch. Maybe without live update and such, who cares.

Oh well, I'm still using the mini-commander applet instead of that abomination called deskbar. I might as well do the same to with the panel...

---

If those people that can't use it stick with Lucid (it being an LTS and all), by the time support for Lucid is dropped those computers will be what, 12 years old or something?  Maybe more? Yes, it would be better to not need compositing but it's not the end of the world if it is required.

Did you notice that Gnome-shell is incompatible with VMs?  And, yes, there are plenty of people who use those.
Note also that some intel integrated graphics are rather low-end.
Then there's the question of what happens when someone tries running, say, Google Earth; if 2 apps use the same resource, both run slower.  

On my own netbook made last year, 3-d graphics feel sluggish when I use them.
Compositing  also will not help battery life, which is something Ubuntu has been behind on.


Gnome-shell requires 3d, while Compiz works with 2d. 
If Compiz has more effects, and gnome-shell is incompatible...I say, Compiz is by far the better choice, with lower hardware requirements, more effects, and better speed.

Ibidem

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2010-01-15
> **ranch hand said:**
> ```

gksudo gedit /etc/apt/sources.list

```

If you are adding these things manually, it would be preferred not to add this to your sources list, instead create a new .list file in /etc/apt/sources.list.d:

```
sudo touch /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ricotz-testing.list && sudo nano /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ricotz-testing.list
```

Or something like:

```
sudo sh -c 'echo "deb http://ppa.launchpad.net/ricotz/testing/ubuntu karmic main" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ricotz-testing.list'
```

Later, Seeker

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2010-01-16
> **ranch hand said:**
> 
You should still use "gksudo" instead of "sudo" for graphic apps.

I wasn't buying that, but I see the problem now.

It's not that you shouldn't use sudo for graphical apps, but that sudo doesn't reset $HOME to the home directory of the target user by default, which may cause files owned by root to be written to your home directory.

Using the 'H' option seems to take care of that, 'sudo -H *some_command*'. Of course this works for graphical apps if you use GDM as your display manager but you then have an issue if you use KDM or other display manager that starts the X session with overly restrictive options that will then require using gksudo/kdesudo to run the graphical app anyway or figure out some additional sudo option.

Later, Seeker

---

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-16
> **qamelian said:**
> I use Alt-tab to switch between apps anyway. Even on "old" Gnome, I never used a window switcher. I don't expect anyone else to understand, but even when I tried running a dock, I could never find a reason to actually use it for anything...ever. Sorry, but that's just the way it is. :)

Hey man, to each his own. I'm just not too sure about this whole GS thing. I feel like it's 1 step forward, 4 steps back.

For example, we use Ubuntu desktop as one of our servers at work right now. It's virtualized. Well, forget Gnome next time we upgrade. That's out of the picture. Now we have to consider XFCE or KDE running on our server virtually.

I don't know man. I want to like it. I'm trying really hard to justify it. But each time I just go back to KDE and it's like... "aww yes! I get this! This works!" 

I don't plan to discontinue using GS... I just hope it gets a major, major overhaul within the next few months prior to final release.

### Post by qamelian on 2010-01-17
> **Roasted said:**
> Hey man, to each his own. I'm just not too sure about this whole GS thing. I feel like it's 1 step forward, 4 steps back.
Which is actually how I felt about the move from Gnome 1.4 to Gnome 2.X. :)

> For example, we use Ubuntu desktop as one of our servers at work right now. It's virtualized. Well, forget Gnome next time we upgrade. That's out of the picture. Now we have to consider XFCE or KDE running on our server virtually.
That makes no sense. Even when Gnome 3 is released, you aren't forced to use Gnome-shell. The FAQ linked to earlier clearly indicates that the old-style panels are still going to be available and so is Metacity for those who prefer/need them.
> **GNOME Panel**

  
**GNOME won't support the current panel and window manager anymore and I don't want to use GNOME Shell**

 **The GNOME 2.x panel and Metacity (the window manager) will still be available.**
 Downstream distributions such as Fedora, openSUSE and Ubuntu will have the option to include them in their distribution. You will be able to install them just as now you can install sawfish, compiz, etc inside your GNOME session. 

> I don't know man. I want to like it. I'm trying really hard to justify it. But each time I just go back to KDE and it's like... "aww yes! I get this! This works!" 
We'll need to disagree on this one too. KDE 4 is absolutely crap on my systems. even the latest stable release is far to sluggish on my hardware and it is not friendly twoard the laptop I use at all. Gnome-shell runs slick and smooth on my aging Compaq Presario with its ATI Mobility Radeon 9000 GPU and 1GB of RAM. KDE by comparison drags on all fronts, and runs a minimum of 8 degrees hotter while idling. KDE is pretty, but it's unusable on both my main systems and most of the systems I've successfully tested Gnome-shell on. I'd rather cut off my own right arm than spend a day working in KDE.

> I don't plan to discontinue using GS... I just hope it gets a major, major overhaul within the next few months prior to final release.
I expect you'll see a lot of changes in the coming months. Things are constantly being reexamined and fine-tuned by the developers. I've been using Gnome-shell exclusively on my production laptop for months and I absolutely will not go back to working without it unless something I truly find to be better comes along. At the moment, there really isn't anything better that suits my needs.

### Post by TheStatsMan on 2010-01-17
I have recently been trying out gnome shell. Until I tried it out I thought people were just complaining because it is different and new. Now that I have tried it out I don't really like it. Not only does it look good it seems pretty responsive. Mac's look good and work well for people that like them. Gnome on the other hand is a bit stuck in the past, but is this the future?

### Post by qamelian on 2010-01-17
> **Roasted said:**
> I really, really would like to see one of your systems. I hate to take a stab here but I have installed KDE4 on countless systems, and NONE have been such a traumatizing experience as you describe. Ever. Even Pentium 4 512mb RAM systems with integrated graphics. Each one has worked great. *sigh*

Which system would you like to see? I have access to over 100 test boxes scattered around the sites of various clients for whom I provide support. KDE works fine on some of them, but not on others. At a rough estimate, I'd say it works smoothly on roughly 40% of my clients test boxes. It runs like crap on both my aging laptop and on my Phenom X3 desktop. The Phenom has an ATI Radeon 2400HD GPU and 3GB of RAM. KDE is jerky...GS is smooth. 

I used to be a big KDE fan, but honestly, I haven't seen a release on any distro that I would call stable after 2.2. The 3.X series is what converted me to a Gnome user.

Anyway, one of the nicest things about Linux in my opinion is that you really can find a way to make it fit almost anyone. If KDE is what you like and it works well for you, that's cool. I just can get any work done on it, while the increase in my productivity on Gnome-shell is actually measurable. :)

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-17
> **qamelian said:**
> while the increase in my productivity on Gnome-shell is actually measurable. :)

I wish I could say the same.

GS is what drove me to KDE... fortunately I know if GS lands and it sucks, I at least have a solid option that works for me.

Likewise, I work in a school district, where there is roughly 1500 systems. I certainly didn't install Kubuntu on all of them, since we are (sadly) an all windows (on desktops) network. But I wouldn't be surprised if I installed Kubuntu on at least a couple dozen systems in my 1.5 years working there. Maybe not quite 100 like you quoted, but certainly more than 30.

The sad thing is, the machines I installed Kubuntu on were older rigs that aren't used in production anymore, such as that 512mb RAM P4 one I spoke about earlier. So it's not like I have a triple or quad core to throw at them... they're all old boxes ready to get kicked out of the building, and yet they work just fine. I won't be unfair here, if I activate compositing on those boxes (some of them have awful intel integrated cards) then I would no doubt have slower load times, slower launch times for applications, etc. But once I de-activate compositing, or only activate compositing on machines with a somewhat decent graphics card, then I notice a decent speed improvement - a speed improvement that rivals Gnome 2.X (I install Ubuntu on about as many machines as I've installed Kubuntu).

I never had GS lag on me. GS has been pretty consistent in terms of speed. I've had a ton of crashes, in fact some crashes happened when I was idle (leaning back in the chair watching TV for 5 minutes+ and blam, GS crashed). But it's to be expected with its current testing stage. It's completely unfair for me to tell alpha software that they're unstable. :P 

I don't mean to be a smartass with what I say, but there's no possibility that KDE is slow on all of those machines. There's graphic card drivers that could pertain to its lag among other things. But there's no way KDE sucks on all of your machines and works fine on my 50+ machines. There's a curve ball to the situation, and "KDE is just slow" isn't the answer. If it was slow, it'd be slow everywhere. But it's not. I have 13 second boot times with Kubuntu on my work laptop - almost on par with Ubuntu on the same machine. I say almost because I clocked Ubuntu at 15 seconds.

Like I said before, I'm a 5 year old Gnome user and a KDE user of only a few months (maybe 3 months?). I'm trying to like GS. I find myself digging for a way to say "This. I like this." But each time I'm just left frustrated with the slow speed I'm able to accomplish small tasks. 

Your opinion gives me some gleam of hope with GS, in terms of maybe it's just me being frustrated and not giving it enough time to really shine. I'm going to install it on a spare machine of mine, since I just put Kubuntu on my work laptop and I need (not to sound like a smartass) stability so I can get work done. A work laptop isn't really the proper grounds to test alpha software. :P :P 

I look forward to playing around with the notification system. I feel like that's the one piece of the puzzle that'll make or break GS entirely. But after all, we're talking about Gnome here. If anybody can pull it off, it's them. Let's hope...

---

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-17
> **OlyPerson said:**
> Pretty good writing skills for a five year old I must say!

On a serious note, what can't you do as fast?  Which small tasks?

When I wrote that, I had to think twice, but I figured I'd let it. :P 

It's not that GS is a bad idea. It just takes away the quick fundamentals behind multitasking. I honestly think the notification system will help in this department, but I'll speak my opinion on it anyway.

When I get an instant message, but firefox is full screened, how do I know? The answer is, I don't know. You don't know when a window needs attention.

If the notification system operates in a fashion that a dock would, GS just might work out very nicely. The overlay is very attractive, it's speed is consistent from machine to machine, new or old. It's application system that was changed to accomodate ALL programs into 1 menu alphabetically I really like. I like how they did away with the "more" button and made the entire "Applications" menu one giant button. All very positive and much needed changes.

I just hope the notification system does the last thing that drives me insane. It's not only a pain in the *** to have to activate the overlay, click on the IM, then the overlay zooms out, but it's also a pain to not know when I get that IM. 

But like I said, if the notification system resembles a dock, this might be a hit.

What I'd like to see in the notification system is some sort of configurability as well.

I want to have the option to show the preview of the message I just got or not. There are times I might be getting an instant message from one person, but I don't want it to be public for all eyes to see that are around my computer via notifications. Sometimes I just want the option to have a little icon bounce up and down to notify me I got a message, instead of showing me the ACTUAL message. I'd also like to be able to click that icon, and the instant message pop up as the primary window.

In essence, like I probably said 3 or 4 times now, a small, very functional "dock-like" system.

If GS can fit this bill, they just might have me sold. But ALT-Tabing, while very nice, is not the answer. Nor is the answer "oh just activate the overlay and click on the IM." I LOVE the overlay. I use it in KDE all the time. But sometimes I just need to see that flashing icon so I can click it and blam - I can do my thing accordingly.

Simple things, but they're a big deal to me - and I'm sure they'd be a big deal to other users. That's actually the argument I hear among the users I showed GS - all of which gave it a thumbs down. I'm sure if something like this is implemented, it might just be a smexy and rather functional desktop, and something those same users would thereby approve over.

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-17
> **OlyPerson said:**
> In my opinion the alphabetical list was a bad move, makes finding something if you don't exactly remember the name very difficult and windows-ish.

Well, the one thing I always thought that sucked about Gnome was there was never a real method to organize your programs that were newly installed. There were times I'd download a program and it'd be under system tools. Other times internet. Other times preferences. And OTHER times administration. Very poor way to organize applications. This may not entirely be Gnome's fault, but even still, it's a problem that pissed me off since day 1 on Gnome, yet was non existent (for some reason) in KDE.

But I have an obvious question to ask to the GS developers - why make alphabetizing mandatory? Why not give us the option?

This is Linux. Land of customizing. It'd be nice to have an option to have the sub categories OR alphabetizing the menu like it currently is.

Question for you OlyPerson - is your notification system not entirely implemented? I have it too, but it's kind of lackluster in its current state. Also - anybody running GS on a dual screen setup? The notification system is weird on dual screen setups....

I mean just look at the personal preferences from me to you to other users here. If we could get several options available, that'd be just plain fricken awesome.

Can you imagine something like... the GS Control Panel? Which controls a lot of basic functions of how GS functions and how the notification system works?

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-17
> **OlyPerson said:**
> That would be awesome if something like that was implemented, I'd like there to be one place to be able to configure all of that.  They could make it like the Compiz settings manager or something like that.

What do you think about the sidebar?  What do you think they should do with that?

I'd like to see the sidebar act as a side-loaded dock. Perhaps it could be split into 2 sections with a small divider. The top half (or whichever) could be quick launch icons to launch firefox, thunderbird, or whatever other applications - in the exact same manner you would have had with adding quick launch icons to the top panel of Gnome's 2.X series. That way you at least retain similar functionality of the previous 2.X series. Then the bottom half could be a "dock" section which houses icons of currently active applications.

Check it... you set it up like that and say you have Pidgin running with 5 IMs. You click on the Pidgin icon and blam - Pidgin buddy list + 5 IMs open for you to navigate through - OR (following through with more dock-like functionality) you can right click the Pidgin icon and see the 5 IMs listed there - allowing you to single click on any of them and bring up ONLY the IM you want.

This of course, would be an auto hide thing (with the option of having it staticly mounted). If this was implemented in that manner, it would most definitely add the efficiency of multitasking to GS - something it severely lacks at the moment. The overlay should NOT, absolutely NOT be "the" method to switch from window to window - but meerly a way to manage where windows are organized in certain workspaces.

---

My impression from the mailing list has been
[list=1]

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-18
> **Merk42 said:**
> Has any non-coder requested something in the GNOME Shell mailing list that actually **has gotten implemented**?

My impression from the mailing list has been
[list=1]
[*]Person has idea for feature FOO
[*]Other people like the idea for feature FOO
[*]GNOME dev comes in and says "well that doesn't fit with our vision, we want BAR" and then the thread dies[/list]

Well, yes and no.

I was on the mailing list because when I first saw GS there were several things I thought everybody else would jump on, but I wanted to do my part as an open source fanatic and offer suggestions. My first suggestion was when the "more" button was still around, next to applications.

I posted on the mailing list and said it'd be much easier for users to utilize the entire "Applications" section as a single button, instead of having the little "more" button as the means of opening additional applications. The response I got back was not snobby or anything, but it was something along the lines of "Well, unfortunately that button has gotten smaller." That's the day I put Kubuntu on my computer. :P 

However, to my surprise I got GS installed via PPA about 7 or 8 weeks later and blam - no more "more" button - the entire applications bar was one giant button.

Not to mention, other things have been added/tweaked that I really like. Seeing what has changed in the last 2 months gives me positive reinforcement that GS might be a true home run, especially considering how (relatively) stable it is now and the fact that there's still months to go in development.

So to answer your question, yes I did get a reaction like that - but in the end it was changed anyway.

---

### Post by qamelian on 2010-01-18
> **Roasted said:**
> I don't mean to be a smartass with what I say, but there's no possibility that KDE is slow on all of those machines. There's graphic card drivers that could pertain to its lag among other things. But there's no way KDE sucks on all of your machines and works fine on my 50+ machines. There's a curve ball to the situation, and "KDE is just slow" isn't the answer. If it was slow, it'd be slow everywhere. But it's not. I have 13 second boot times with Kubuntu on my work laptop - almost on par with Ubuntu on the same machine. I say almost because I clocked Ubuntu at 15 seconds.
You're wrong. It does suck on all those machines. I'm not some noob who doesn't know what to check or troubleshoot. This is my profession and it is something I have developed a reputation for doing well. The affected machines are running a variety of hardware configurations and there are many potential causes. In some cases, I've been able to alleviate performance problems by disabling core pieces of KDE functionality. Which piece needs to be disabled varies from one set of machines to the next. One some machines, performance is just poor. Period. As I mentioned before it works fine on most of the 100+ test boxes, but is totally unacceptable on about 40% of them. I really don't care what you believe. Those are the facts. I can't speak for the systems you administer, but I know the ones I handle for my clients inside and out.

### Post by 23meg on 2010-01-18
> **Merk42 said:**
> Has any non-coder requested something in the GNOME Shell mailing list that actually **has gotten implemented**?

My impression from the mailing list has been
[list=1]
[*]Person has idea for feature FOO
[*]Other people like the idea for feature FOO
[*]GNOME dev comes in and says "well that doesn't fit with our vision, we want BAR" and then the thread dies[/list]

Has any *coder* requested something in the mailing list that actually has gotten implemented? 

Could it be that the problem lies with the notion of limiting oneself to *requesting* people to implement arbitrary features of your liking or habit that may or may not fit into their vision for their project, rather than implementing them yourself, or getting/paying someone to implement them for you, on top of the open code and frameworks that the project has provided?

Having a clear, persistent vision for a project, and saying "no" if that's what it takes to preserve that vision, however out of fashion it may be these days, is a good thing, especially if your system is one that excels at accommodating disagreement, by providing people who disagree with the same tools and resources as those who don't to produce alternatives to their liking, while still benefiting from the work they disagree with.

---

### Post by LiquidMeson on 2010-01-18
> **23meg said:**
> 
Having a clear, persistent vision for a project, and saying "no" if that's what it takes to preserve that vision, however out of fashion it may be these days, is a good thing, especially if your system is one that excels at accommodating disagreement, by providing people who disagree with the same tools and resources as those who don't to produce alternatives to their liking, while still benefiting from the work they disagree with.

Your absolutely right, and being in a capitalistic society I should have no grip with your contention. However as it stands, in order for open source to work and be successful it must allow for co-operation off all ideas no matter how small.

The notion that ideas should be handed down through a hierarchy like that of an old news paper business are nearing their end, as newer system of global brainstorming are emerging. Websites such as digg and reddit have shown and proven this functionality.

---

### Post by 23meg on 2010-01-18
> **LiquidMeson said:**
> as it stands, in order for open source to work and be successful it must allow for co-operation off all ideas no matter how small.

I agree, and none of what I said contradicts that.

Maintaining a compatible branch of GNOME Shell with your changes to parts of its design that you disagree with (maybe even using the [extensions system]("http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell/Extensions")), while still participating in its development discourse and contributing to the project, would be a good example of cooperation *plus* disagreement. Limiting your participation to feature requests, and crying foul when your requests find no response because they're incompatible with the vision of the project (I'm not aiming this at anyone in particular; it's just a common pattern), is a bad example. 

> **LiquidMeson said:**
> The notion that ideas should be handed down through a hierarchy like that of an old news paper business are nearing their end, as newer system of global brainstorming are emerging. Websites such as digg and reddit have shown and proven this functionality.

I would be more cautious when arguing that a particular system is completely replacing an old one, as opposed to displacing, changing and mixing with it, to different degrees in different areas, and when comparing Digg and Reddit to software development discourse. We're surely going through radical changes in public discourse and the way information is shared, but the new paradigm has its own downsides, and its effects are not global, and don't apply to every field in the same way.

---

### Post by ronacc on 2010-01-18

co-operation and accommodation are well and good but remember a hippopotamus is a horse designed by a committee . There needs to be a defining vision or the result is chaos . You have a very small view of "open source" , we don't all need to co-operate on an single project that is why for instance there isn't one media player there are many .

---

### Post by LiquidMeson on 2010-01-18
> **23meg said:**
> 
I would be more cautious when arguing that a particular system is completely replacing an old one, as opposed to displacing, changing and mixing with it

Merely trying to hammer in the point. There never will be an either or.

> **ronacc said:**
> 
a hippopotamus is a horse designed by a committee

ns quote. however a committee is paid to be involved and therefore must leave a mark. Where as in open source, those who are interested are involved, and their mark is a solid product.

my grip with GNOME Shell, was that for such a large project that inevitably effects a large group of people, only a small subset of people were able decide upon the resulting image. Yes I recognize that these people are incredibly good at what they do, and are far better programmers then most. However for such a project all stages should allow for input, even if that input is in theory/media/etc and not literal code. 

I'm talking about broadening the input beyond just those who can make commits and branches. Collected discussion should decide the outcome. And while the chaos of such input might be harder to work with, the outcome is far more beneficial to a greater amount of people. 

Food for thought: [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VBkWt-PXPJo](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VBkWt-PXPJo)
Full video: [http://www.mininova.org/tor/3040794](http://www.mininova.org/tor/3040794)

Its not just the Internet or open-source, its that these pieces allowed people to participate. Made it easy to participate. People who never though they could contribute before have now helped to make websites such as wikipedia successful. A website now fundamental to the Internet and human knowledge.

People have allows cared, they were just never able to show it before.

### Post by k64 on 2010-01-23
My advice:

Step 1: ```
sudo apt-get autoremove gnome-shell
```

After step 1, [Step 2]('apt:/gnome-shell').

Edit: Another would-be problem: Do you have proprietary drivers installed that enable compositing? Compositing is **required** by GNOME Shell.

---

### Post by k64 on 2010-01-23
> **libihero said:**
> the gnome shell i use is really buggy, everytime i run i get:
```
WARNING: Application calling GLX 1.3 function "glXCreatePixmap" when GLX 1.3 is not supported!  This is an application bug!
      JS LOG: Failed to acquire org.freedesktop.Notifications; trying again

```
clicking on activities makes the screen go almost black also

This means that you don't have the proper graphics drivers, and that GLX is not supported by your graphics card. You need to get a new graphics card to use GNOME Shell.

### Post by k64 on 2010-01-23
> **libihero said:**
> so the graphics card i currently have wont ever work with gnome shell??
that blows... i got this laptop only a year and a half ago :(

What's the graphics card's model number?

Integrated graphics before nVidia 9000 series and ATI HD 3000 series are absolutely screwed.

I have a discrete graphics card, and it does work. However, it's a PCI Express (ATI Radeon HD 2400 Pro PCIE). Integrated graphics, in contrast, does not have any discrete memory. It has to use system memory, which hampers performance. The only reason my Acer Aspire One works is that it's using the generic VESA driver.

I have a discrete graphics card, and it does work. However, it's a PCI Express (ATI Radeon HD 2400 Pro PCIE). Integrated graphics, in contrast, does not have any discrete memory. It has to use system memory, which hampers performance. The only reason my Acer Aspire One works is that it's using the generic VESA driver.
Thanks for insulting the people with 5 year old laptops using Intel graphics that can run gnome-shell flawlessly.

### Post by bash on 2010-01-24
Been using the shell now since quite a while. Mostly on and off as it's still to unstable and crashes to often for me to use it productivly. 

My impressions so far have been positive and I quite like working with the shell workflow idea. I especially notice that I prefer the shell idea when I'm back in an GNOME 2 environemnt and everything just feels more complicated. It is though a strange feeling at first to not have a classical taskbar. But IMO once you get used to the overlay idea you don't really miss it.

What I'm missing most atm besides stability (It's an alpha/beta version so that is to be expected) is polish. I find that the usability is there, but look wise it I find that most UI elements look they were quickly implemented like first concepts or tests. The font choices are not particularly appealing, things like the right side of the overlay are overly cluttered, and buttons look quite cheap. For example why does there have to be so many recent documents entries? Half would still be more than enough, but would allow for a lot nicer spacing of the three blocks, so that everything wouldn't look so cramped anymore. But well maybe that is still coming and they are first waiting for all features and ideas to be settled on and implemented.

But that brings me to my other point I wonder about: time. Currently the "realese date" is set for the yearly fall realese. I'm not sure if that is enough time to finish up deciding what and how should be implemented, then implementing it, then polishing it, then making it stable and all they while doing "Q&A" to find and squish any remaining bugs. Looking at the current rate of developement I find this quite an ambitouis time frame. And sadly it doesn't look like they have some extra manpower hidden somewhere that they can quickly call for extra help. This is not meant against the developers. I think they are doing good work. But as always so many people can only do so much. Bottom line; I' wondering if the current time frame is realistic.

---

> Any constructive criticism?  I personally like it the more I use it, and it's not even near completion so don't get completely set on your dislike.

I like everything about it, except how dizzy I get using it. I've said a few times in this thread, perhaps if the notification system gets implemented full bore and improved, it would make GS actually usable. We'll see what happens as time progresses towards the final release.

### Post by FatalChaos on 2010-01-25
> **Roasted said:**
> I'm still not fully understanding a few things. I hear they're facts, other say they're myths, etc. 

1 - Will shell be Gnome's default? Or will the panels be default?
2 - Will Gnome ever abandon the panels, or will they remain regardless of shell's success?

As it currently stands, the shell is slated to become Gnome's default DE in september 2010 (Gnome 3.0), meaning Gnome will abandon the panels. Of course, someone could always fork the current gnome panel DE. 

Personally, I like some of gnome-shell, like how it takes care of workspaces (although i don't know how to change the default number of workspaces, or if that's even possible), but I can't really work w/ gnome-shell unless I have a panel or dock, and currently gnome-shell isn't efficient enough memory/cpu/gpu wise for me to use w/ awn.

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-25
> **FatalChaos said:**
> As it currently stands, the shell is slated to become Gnome's default DE in september 2010 (Gnome 3.0), meaning Gnome will abandon the panels. Of course, someone could always fork the current gnome panel DE. 

Personally, I like some of gnome-shell, like how it takes care of workspaces (although i don't know how to change the default number of workspaces, or if that's even possible), but I can't really work w/ gnome-shell unless I have a panel or dock, and currently gnome-shell isn't efficient enough memory/cpu/gpu wise for me to use w/ awn.

Well, I have at least seen from the Gnome site that as of the release of Shell, they won't abandon panels right away, and I read elsewhere that panels will be default at least for now, even in sept 2010, but that's as far as I found. I was just curious if they plan to keep them around.

It's not that I'm trying to down GS, cause I do like it, but if they drop panels they will receive so much grief from the community. It'd make a lot of sense to upkeep the panels with minor feature add-ins and minor bug fixes for the future for those with older hardware. Argue it all you want, but GS won't be as old-hardware friendly as panels - despite how friendly GS seems to be thus far.

I also think people are taking the GS workspace switcher being so amazing vs panels a little too over the top. Anybody ever click and drag a window to the edge of the screen? It goes to the other workspace. OGMAWD! Just like you do in the GS overlay mode.

Looking forward to seeing what GS brings. Hopefully they bring something to the table that relieves my eyes of the strain I receive after using the zoom in/out continuously that I currently have to on GS. I'm sure it'll be greatly improved in time though.

---

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-25
I'd just like to add, after reading about some users commenting on the recent updates of GS, I installed it on my newly re-done Ubuntu work laptop. Man I like what they did with the option to change the view of workspace layouts. That actually makes using multiple workspaces (yet using the overlay as an alternative to alt-tab) much more practical since having multiple workspaces activated makes windows EXTREMELY small if you have multiple workspaces open.

Very nice...

Question - 

Is there any way you can change the key binding of activating the overlay from the super key to something else? 

Also is sthere a way to change the key binding of alt-tab to something else? It'd be kind of cool to try out using these extra mouse buttons as one of those functions.

I know it's early yet for the notification system, but is there a way to extend the amount of time the notification for instant messages are displayed on the screen? 

And yet another thought - it'd be nice if I could click on the notification that comes up to make it the active window... possible yet?

Has GS functionality changed at all for dual screen monitors? Last I used GS on my desktop it was kind of meh. It's slick on my laptop though...

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-26
Honest question to you hardcore GS users - Do you miss your panel at all? Like do you not find it difficult to sift through open windows? Fire up Pidgin and begin conversations with 5 people. Do you guys not find it difficult and cumbersome to use at that point? And no - ALT + Tab is not a valid answer.

I tried the sidebar today, and while I liked it, I hate how when it extends out, it forces the rest of the screen to resize. It'd be much smoother if it would just plop on top since it would retract back in anyway. (I'm talking about the sidebar in the more minimal sized state).

Another -1 for the sidebar was it could only act as a launcher. I already had skype open. When I went to the sidebar to click skype (in an attempt to feel GS in a more user friendly manner) it launched a 2nd instance of Skype instead of opening the existing one.

I'm trying to like it. But certain things keep dragging it down yet. I know it's still in development but, does anybody else feel this way too?

---

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-26
> **qamelian said:**
> I'm afraid Alt-tab *is* a valid answer as it is how I've always moved between windows. I don't miss the panel at all. Your dislike for an option is not enough to render that option invalid. And I don't find it cumbersome at all. I can easily Alt-tab through my windows in less time than it takes me to reach for the mouse and navigate to a button on the panel.


Let me shed some light on something I'm seeing here:

> **qamelian said:**
> I'm afraid Alt-tab *is* a valid answer as it is how **I've always moved between windows.**

 **I don't miss the panel at all.** 

 **And I don't find it cumbersome at all. **

**I can easily Alt-tab through my windows in less time than it takes me to reach for the mouse and navigate to a button on the panel.**


Your entire argument is based on your opinion of it. My argument is based, not only on my opinion of it, but the simple fact that it's the over-welming response that pretty much everybody is giving GS.

There's 2 things I hear CONSTANTLY about Gnome Shell that people complain about.

1 - People hate the zoom. Some people just don't like it and don't want to have to zoom in and out every time. Understandable, but a minor matter of preference.

2 - No easy way to navigate through open windows. Alt-Tab may work for you, but I feel as though I can say it is not a valid response to the lack of a panel based on the simple fact most everybody has the same opinion I do.

I'm not trying to step on any toes here. Your opinion is your opinion, my opinion is my opinion. But the reality is simple: It's a problem, and it needs to be addressed or the masses of GS users will give it the thumbs down and either use 3.0 with panels, 2.X, or a fork of Gnome that will undoubtedly come about if certain things aren't implemented in a way the users like.

If Alt-Tab is your thing, nobody is stopping you from using it. But that argument won't convince the mass user-base. I'm sure of that much.

I recommend grabbing a PPA of the latest version. The repo version is old. Have a looksie at the Ricotz PPA. That's where I got Gnome Shell. It comes with frequent updates where as the repo version... ehh. 

[http://ubuntu-tweak.com/source/ricotz-testing/](http://ubuntu-tweak.com/source/ricotz-testing/)

Secondly, if you want it to auto start, go to startup applications and create a new launcher for Gnome Shell. The command is:

gnome-shell --replace

That way Gnome Shell will take over immediately when logging in.

### Post by qamelian on 2010-01-27
> **Roasted said:**
> You're assuming I'm basing this off of the forum alone. I've done a ton of research, read a lot of reviews, and showed a lot of people Gnome Shell in the last few months. What I've seen is based on what most users are saying, both to me, to other forums, to this forum, friends of mine who have seen it, co-workers, and even the Gnome Shell newsletter.

All I'm saying is, Gnome Shell still needs work, and that's okay because it has months to go. It is absolutely not in it's "ready state" as of today. 

And that's fine that your users seem to like Gnome Shell, but I find it weird that your users you conducted tests with are also in direct line to what your personal opinion is.

I'm not trying to step on any toes. I'm just calling it like it is.
I never said that Gnome-shell doesn't still require work. Again (god, I'm sick of saying this), if you have bothered at all to read my posts in this thread as well as one or two others relating to Gnome-shell, I have agreed with others on some things that still need(ed) work. 

If the users I've conducted usability tests were in "direct line" with my opinion, I would expect the numbers to be even higher than they are. Whether or not you believe the numbers or not makes no difference to me. You aren't a user who relies on me for support. 

The tests I have been conducting directly relate to my ability to support my clients and recommend appropriate work environments for them. Knowing that Gnome-shell is on its way, I would be remiss not to test it extensively in the organizations I support. If the tests indicate that Gnome-shell will have a negative impact on my clients, then I am responsible for ensuring that GS does not become a part of their work environment. By the same token, if the test results prove that GS will benefit my clients, I need to be prepared to provide appropriate training during the transition for those not involved in the usability tests.

I take this testing very seriously. A bad decision/recommendation on my part regarding GS could negatively impact my customers and by extension reduce my own income. If you think I might be anxious to throw away clients and, as a result, my own sources of income, I urge you to think again.

### Post by qamelian on 2010-01-27
> **Merk42 said:**
> I'm sure the majority of your clients do naturally like it.

I'm just saying that this thread is for the discussion likes and dislikes of the shell.
I'm well aware of what parts of the shell your clients like, I'd like to know what parts of the shell they dislike.

The overall dark appearance is rather unpopular with all but a few of them. The linear approach to adding and removing workspaces feels a little clumsy to many of them, although they like the concept of dynamically adding and removing workspaces, in general. There are others, to be sure, but we haven't compiled a comprehensive list as yet, although we will be completing a survey within the next few weeks which will give the members of the test groups the chance to detail their specific pros & cons on a per user basis.

When we initially began testing, the percentage of users who were in favour of GS was much smaller than it is now. We almost didn't continue testing past the third week, in fact. What prompted us to continue testing was a survey of the existing testers which indicated that the number of positive reactions was increasing. At the end of the first week, only about 6% of my users liked GS. Another 29% were indifferent. The remaining 65% actively disliked it. The same survey conducted at the end of week 3 showed that the percentage of users who liked GS had risen to 23%, while the indifferent group dropped to 26%. The remainder continued to dislike it. This convinced us that some of the initial dislike was simply discomfort because GS was different and unfamiliar in some ways. The continued decline in dissenters seems to bear this out. We still have a few users who actively dislike Gnome-shell, but when asked to rate the existing Gnome environment, almost half of them also disliked it.

---

### Post by uBeer on 2010-01-27
> **ranch hand said:**
> I would really like t obe able to get the sucker to come up without freezing my system.  I just gave it another whack with the repo version and lost the entire desktop, black screen.  tty1 did get a reboot for me.

What in flinderation is this;
crap.

Every thing I am doing is FOSS.  Why drm?  How do  you set the "mode"?  Search turns up nothing of value that I can see.

I did insert a xorg.conf file calling for uxa as that is supposed to help with the radeon cards (mine, I do not know about) and the screen seemed fine with the compiz manager when I booted up.

I think that the pc that you were using has an ATI card?
Then this > Try adjusting the vblank_mode configuration parameter.
do_wait: drmWaitVBlank returned -1, IRQs don't seem to be working correctly
only works with the 2.6.33 kernel. The drm stands for Direct Rendering Manager. 
And on [Phoronix - link]("http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=Nzc4OQ") you can read a bit more about IRQ and the new kernel.

But the bottom line is: nevermind this message as it's quite harmless.

> **Roasted said:**

Well generally I just type the file name of whatever I want into the search bar after pressing the super button, but when I don't do that I just use the terminal (which is hot-keyed to Ctrl+alt+x for me) and when I don't do that I use Docky and if I don't do that I just go to overlay and click on it.

> **k3lt01 said:**
> I don't understand how using a mouse, or touchpad on a laptop, is harder than Alt-tabbing. You slide the mouse/touchpad in one easy movement and click/tap so its 2 movements. Alt-tabbing is a new movement for each thing or at least it was in my attempt to use G-S a few weeks ago.

You only have to move your pinky about 1cm and your thumb the same distance if you have your hand on the keyboard already.  To use the trackpad you move your hand down and swipe a couple times to position it right the go back and click.  I'm being honest when I say that alt-tabbing is at least 10 times faster.  

It's not that using your mouse is more difficult, it just takes much, much long to do tasks- which is why file navigation (and other stuff) is a lot faster in the terminal if you can type fast.

### Post by texaswriter on 2010-01-28
Merk42> It looks nice, but I like the current Gnome + Compiz just fine... Unnecessary changes that are shirked by its users should not be implemented unless it is a necessary step.. Being unnecessary kind of precludes the necessity.. Therefore **it is fine the way it is**. 

Gnome plus Compiz is more variety... Can have Gnome or Gnome + Compiz... user picks... Now, user NO PICK. Big difference there. 

I'll probably try it, but given tradeoffs, I'd likely reinstall something else... Given that will be somewhat down the road, I'll probably pick another distro that supports my current hardware [or current one with XFce or KDE]. 

I've noticed something about various programmers: Programmers rarely take into consideration users **and** they rarely over test their programs / code well enough. If they'd do those things programs would be much better, instead what we get are things out of tune with what is wanted/needed, and things that don't work like they are supposed. That's not specific to anybody, just general to the programming community. If I started today trying to list things, I wouldn't finish ever!! 
 
So, I'm using this an example of why, after seeing virtually *no* positive posts ANYWHERE, it's probably a bad idea to take away whatever is being taken away and to force onto what is not wanted... That's like saying, how can i help you today... not tell you what, you can have only this... take it or leave it. 

I mean seriously, if you are currently disaffected/dissatisfied with GNOME, I can see you liking it, but otherwise this change will change the fundamental dynamics that separate [them]. There certainly are replacements for GNOME, don't think forcing a change on an unwilling user base won't have consequences..
 
And please don't take this as a complaint at all or even an attack... I've seen the changes it makes, I've heard plenty of comments and stories. And under no circumstance would I get the feeling that this is wanted or a good idea... Gnome works great now... It has the further option of being extended with Compiz... This is a popular solution. Let's leave it at that.

BEcause that is what most of this is: whining. I see very little in form of constructive criticism and very much in the "things are good as they are"-department. While I agree that things are pretty good as they are, there might be something I haven't tried that is better.

I'm not really sure where you were going with that, but I agree entirely 100% with what texas said. Daring to do things that users don't like isn't adventurous. It's stupid. 

Just by reading comments and looking at screenshots, anybody can figure out that a lot of choice in customization, as of now, is completely gone.

I've been trying to force myself to use GS. And for who? For what? Why? What I have works great. Why do I need to adapt to a new interface? Why do I have to use Alt Tab to switch through active applications? Why do I have to use zoom effects? 

All this time when using GS I've felt like a puzzle piece that won't fit, and a 5 year old is soaking me in water and trying to beat the puzzle piece in place. While I do like the interface of GS, when I use it on my work laptop, I get so irritated with it at times and find myself closing it each time and breathing a sigh of relief when I see the panels again.

I see a bright future for Gnome if they continue to push panels by default, and allow Shell as the add-in 3d eye candy, much how like Compiz is now. But Texas nailed it dead on. We're not Windows users here. We have the power to easily change to something else. Ironically, in doing some reading about Gnome Shell, I found an interview with Shuttleworth where he explained that he wants Kubuntu and Ubuntu to work hand-in-hand and be completely parallel with each other in terms of features, implementations, etc. If GS bombs, and Kubuntu is picking up more speed, 2 and 2 is 4 here guys. ;) 

This will either go VERY good for Gnome, or very, very bad. I can't see there being any in-between.

I am not saying that GS will be incredibly much better than the ancient "panels in the bottom and/or top"-approach we've lived with for a while now. > **'[h2o] said:**
> 
which does not mean that it won't be in the future. What's your point?


*ahem*

> **roasted said:**
> 
just by reading comments and looking at screenshots, anybody can figure out that a lot of choice in customization, **_as of now_**, is completely gone.


> **'[h2o] said:**
> 

You don't. You can simply continue using the old Gnome 2.x panels if that is what you like. Also, if the majority of the Gnome users are averted by the introduction of GS I assume that it will simply be dropped or changed accordingly. 

I just wanted to chime in and say that changing paradigms, which is what GS is attempting, is hard and always met with some resistance. But if the new paradigm is better than the previous paradigms then eventually people will accept it. I am not saying that GS will be incredibly much better than the ancient "panels in the bottom and/or top"-approach we've lived with for a while now. I am just pointing out that while people might be whining about it being different now, there is nothing that says it can't be just as successful as what we have now.

There's a ton of reasons to keep panels around, and I think Gnome is smart enough to realize this. For example, despite how great 3d compositing on virtual systems may get in the future, what network admin in the right mind would "prefer" to have 3d effects on a server? I know several people, my workplace included, who virtualize desktop versions of Ubuntu (gnome) and use them as servers. 

As long as the panels remain an option, GS will have a much greater time of transitioning into the mainstream. The second we're dropped into a pool of hot water and told, oh, just suck it up, is when bad things will happen.

Not to knock on KDE, but I really think KDE's 4.0 release would be plenty enough for Gnome to take smarter steps to implementing GS to the mainstream. I'm sure this is what will happen.

### Post by texaswriter on 2010-01-28
Yeah, Metacity works great for things that don't work with compositing managers. It's very basic, yet just as functional as it needs to be without being over the top. Compiz Fusion is VERY ATTRACTIVE and works for when I'm just surfing and messing with music and low-resource programs. It makes my computing experience better. I ROUTINELY switch between Metacity and Compiz in the course of a day of computing. When I go play my game on the Desktop, metacity --replace, done... compiz --replace. Just like that. 

Oh, well if you listened to people, Compiz is supposed to be able to let games be visible while both are running.. Gues what??? not true!!!... Guess what, I need to switch between the two. Now you are telling m e I won't have that choice... What if something doesn't work quite right with everything. I mean, Linux is always about getting everything right, usually that has to be done manually. I've never had my ATi work *just right* without doing someting, yeah if I watn just basic driver, but 3d, going to have to install sometihng. 

I don't buy it that a compositing manager is going to satisfy even close to everybody all the time. Taking away the choice is lame. Taking away everything key about Gnome will make it something else, plain and simple. I can appreciate that people are trying to make something more appealing and/or streamlined, but like I said in my previous post: Developers rarely take into consideration the wants, needs, and preferences of its users and taking a look at virtually any professional piece of software will prove it. Even the most popular programs I could name are full of bad programming, poor planning, laughable arrangement, etc. As far as I see it, the best possible scenario of this is that you make Ubuntu more attractive to a small selection of *new* users and scare away a large base of current users. Maybe they'll just goto Kubuntu and it's not so bad, or maybe they just jump ship to another distro or OS altogether. That's dissatisfaction for you in a nutshell. 

So, here is hoping that Gnome finds a way to continue what they are doing as well as integrate more choice as seems to be the Linux way.  I much prefer Gnome, nut KDE, not some other choice I haven't tried, and won't like it if I boot into a new upgrade and find everything so drastically different that I have to relearn how to use my machine...

---

Oh, well if you listened to people, Compiz is supposed to be able to let games be visible while both are running.. Gues what??? not true!!!... Guess what, I need to switch between the two. Now you are telling m e I won't have that choice... What if something doesn't work quite right with everything. I mean, Linux is always about getting everything right, usually that has to be done manually. I've never had my ATi work *just right* without doing someting, yeah if I watn just basic driver, but 3d, going to have to install sometihng. 

I don't buy it that a compositing manager is going to satisfy even close to everybody all the time. Taking away the choice is lame. Taking away everything key about Gnome will make it something else, plain and simple. I can appreciate that people are trying to make something more appealing and/or streamlined, but like I said in my previous post: Developers rarely take into consideration the wants, needs, and preferences of its users and taking a look at virtually any professional piece of software will prove it. Even the most popular programs I could name are full of bad programming, poor planning, laughable arrangement, etc. As far as I see it, the best possible scenario of this is that you make Ubuntu more attractive to a small selection of *new* users and scare away a large base of current users. Maybe they'll just goto Kubuntu and it's not so bad, or maybe they just jump ship to another distro or OS altogether. That's dissatisfaction for you in a nutshell. 

So, here is hoping that Gnome finds a way to continue what they are doing as well as integrate more choice as seems to be the Linux way.  I much prefer Gnome, nut KDE, not some other choice I haven't tried, and won't like it if I boot into a new upgrade and find everything so drastically different that I have to relearn how to use my machine...

When I first saw GS, I felt like I wanted to help out best I could. But there's a problem. I'm not a programmer. I'm just a user. So I signed up to the mailing list to voice my opinion in a constructive, productive manner.

What I got initially were negative feedback from the developers. Most of them excused what I had to say and often times would take the time to reply back to me and in a kind way, tell me in a nutshell, "no."

Fast forward about 8 weeks and I install the PPA of GS, which was said to be much improved and much updated since I last used it. BLAM. Everything I recommended in the mailing list was implemented, as well as things that I never thought of that were improvements as well.

So I will say this about GS - it has evolved quite a bit since I originally used it, and all for the better. I feel that there's still things that need to be implemented, and certain things that need to be refined, but it's come a long way.

Plus, from what I understand, GS is written in javascript. I have no doubt there will be a ton of add-ons that will come later on in the game with Gnome. This can be a very explosive implementation if done right.

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-29
Do you guys think (in time, if ever, etc) we'll be able to customize exactly where we can activate the overlay? For example, I ran Kubuntu for a bit and I enjoyed being able to choose which angle or plane of the screen I have to hit to activate the overlay. It'd be nice if I could make the entire top panel of the screen a way to activate the overlay, so I don't have to hot-corner each time.

It's not that the corner is bad, but my dual screen setup at home has my primary monitor on the right side, which means my 2nd monitor is on the left... which means my activities button is right along both. So for me to hot corner on that setup, it's more difficult. On my laptop I just clock the mouse to the corner - blam, overlay. If I clock the mouse to the corner on my desktop, blam, I'm wayyy over on the other monitor.

That way I don't have to judge my distance at all, I can just push the mouse to the edge as fast as I can and know that the "wall" of the monitor will stop me and activate the overlay as I want. It'd be nice to be able to do this with any plane of the monitor, so users with -any- setup can customize it to their needs.

Small thing, but a nice addition if we were to see it as an option.

---

Im converted! Gnome 2.x to GS is like Windowz to Ubuntu sooooooo different but so much better.

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-29
> **jayg1169 said:**
> Just having a play with GS on my laptop and desktop downloaded from the repos.The UI is amazing! but the performance is awful on my rather ageing 1.5Ghz celeron laptop on the desktop q6600 with its great.

Im converted! Gnome 2.x to GS is like Windowz to Ubuntu sooooooo different but so much better.

Looks like its XFCE for the laptop and GS for the desktop.

It could be the lack of a decent graphics card as opposed to the processor itself. I ran GS on some very old hardware (with somewhat decent gpu's) and it ran surprisingly good.

Just yesterday I installed GS on my spare rig, which is a 3.0ghz Pentium 4 with 1GB of DDR2 RAM. You would think GS would fly, but gosh, it sucked. Onboard intel video. I put in a very, very simple Nvidia graphics card, PCI, 128mb RAM, and enabled the 173 drivers (they were the most updated that came down through Ubuntu's hardware manager) and blam - GS was flyinggggg. 

Just a little food for thought on your situation. Although the Celeron's were not top end proc's, I have found that even older hardware (with a little bit of juice in the graphics card) does well, but a killer proc with an extremely poor graphics card will leave much to be desired.

### Post by Menti on 2010-01-29
> **Roasted said:**
> Do you guys think (in time, if ever, etc) we'll be able to customize exactly where we can activate the overlay? 

This is my biggest fear about GS. The design document provides so many justifications for the smallest design decisions, that it sounds as if they didn't want to allow the user to customize that.

For example, the top position of the activities bar and the top left location of the button. There is extensive reasoning explaining both decisions. I really wonder if they will let me put the activities bar on bottom and *break* their intended logic.

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-29
> **Menti said:**
> This is my biggest fear about GS. The design document provides so many justifications for the smallest design decisions, that it sounds as if they didn't want to allow the user to customize that.

For example, the top position of the activities bar and the top left location of the button. There is extensive reasoning explaining both decisions. I really wonder if they will let me put the activities bar on bottom and *break* their intended logic.

I really can't see Gnome saying hey you can't customize this EVER. I just can't. I don't believe it.

I feel like the Gnome devs are taking a "don't give them too much info" approach.

I work in tech support, and I have to play this same game. If a user is getting a new system and you think you can have it in by friday, don't tell them that. Tell them next month, so that way if something blows up along the way, you still have a cushion to fall back on. If I were to give them an earlier date and things not work out, that puts me in a bad position.

I doubt the Gnome devs want to announce that GS is the end-all be-all of all UI out there right now.

It's in testing stage. It's being worked on. Things that the developers told me "no" about have since been implemented. I just find it hard to believe that GS would be so limited out of the box when it's finally released.

If it gets released tomorrow at the stage it's in now, I'll barf. BUT it's current stage is worlds ahead of what it was just a few weeks ago - paving the way for more features/stability to come.

Trying to stay optimistic here. Let's see what hat-trick these guys pull off.

---

### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-29
I just installed Ubuntu Netbook Remix on a VM, and UNR does something that I think would make a LOT of sense in GS. 

When you hit the menu to open (what we know in GS as "activites") on UNR, you can open applications and whatnot. Pretty much the same deal. But active applications are showed by an icon right next to "Activities" in the top panel.

That top panel aint doing anything anyway, besides holding Activities, the clock, and the logout menu. Why the hell not incorporate the same thing? Or at least the option to do so? That way it will kind of take away some of the zoom in/out effect in case people don't like having to use the overlay to switch applications.

Genius. GS needs this, in my opinion. Very easy, very logical add-in that could make a lot of users (myself included) very happy.
