### Post by Roasted on 2010-01-30
> **Menti said:**
> "Every option has a cost". That's GNOME philosophy. Of course config options will be provided in due time, but which ones? Will they allow me to change precisely that small nuisance that drives me mad? I guess that developers see some design decisions as sensible defaults and others as being carved in stone. My problem is that the design document doesn't separate them clearly.

Anyway I'm not that pessimistic. I just wanted to voice my worst fear aloud.



But despite Gnome's simplistic ideology on the subject, look at how much we can customize Gnome 2.X as it is. If that degree of customizing is blocked off, it's only going to take a stab at Gnome in general. Just last night I was looking at my buddy's Windows 7 laptop. Not bad, not bad. You can do quite a bit in 7. Of course, I kept thinking to myself yep can already do that in Ubuntu, yep that too, yeah that as well. BUT the point is Windows 7 is very customizable, and looks very attractive. Gnome is very customizable, and can be made to look very attractive. If Gnome Shell is locked down to what we see now, that's going to really take away some fun.

I just can't see it happening. I can see them getting 1 method down that's rock solid, stable, and ready to roll. THEN adding features 1 at a time and testing them. 

For what it's worth, you hardcore GS users out there who use docks in conjunction with GS, after seeing Ubuntu Netbook Remix and seeing how much I liked the icons of active windows being in the upper corner just next to the Activities button, I tried it out on my laptop, by moving AWN dock to the top of my screen with enough offset (in AWN advanced preferences) to sit the dock below the top GS panel, along with it being semi transparent when not active. Very nice. I dig having the panel at the top with GS. It was kind of a PITA with it at the bottom since each time I moved the mouse to the bottom of the screen, it made the shadow effect from the notification system come up. Anyway, it gives a good feeling to it if you're already a GS + Dock user.

---

### Post by phenest on 2010-01-31
> **Roasted said:**
> You know, considering Gnome Shell isn't even in pre alpha, it's pretty stable. I run it on my work laptop. It crashes on me now and then, sure... but I just run the replace command and get it going again. 

I've seen finished software less stable than what I've seen from Gnome Shell so far. It's worth commending them on.

I have it set up in gconf under /desktop/gnome/session which is where Gnome suggest.
/desktop/gnome/session/required_components/shell gnome-shell (no need for --replace)
/desktop/gnome/session/required_components_list [shell,filemanager]

That way, if it crashes, it will restart itself.

### Post by OlyPerson on 2010-02-01
> **Roasted said:**
> Question. Is there a way to currently disable the notification system, so when my mouse hits the bottom of the screen I don't get the little bit of a black haze that shows up for the notification panel? I figured since it's not all there, I'd disable it a while so it doesn't come up each time I hit my panel (since I'm using my panel on the bottom again).

It's kind of a hack, but you could probably go to /usr/share/gnome-shell/js/ui and rename/delete notificationDaemon.js.  Not sure if it'll screw up other stuff.

I've been messing around with some of the .js files and the css and I am definitely excited for people to start developing for it.  I think someone should come up with a cool theme using the CSS- doesn't seem too difficult.

EDIT: Ok so I did what I suggested and it kinda messed stuff up so I'd take a different approach, but you get the idea.

EDIT 2: Just go to /usr/share/gnome-shell/theme/gnome-shell.css and comment out of the "message tray" bit (I believe lines 555-574), then restart GS (Alt+F2 then type in restart).

---

### Post by seeker5528 on 2010-02-01

Minor annoyances...

If you don't have Nautilus set to draw the desktop, then you get no wallpaper [Screenshot](http://home.comcast.net/~seeker5528/Screenshot-Gnome3-2.png)

After running the gconf-editor to set Nautilus to draw the desktop I found the icons on the desktop are no longer created by .desktop files in a directory somewhere that can just be deleted, so it's back to gconf-editor again to figure out where that stuff is.

So now if I am on a workspace with no running application or the applications are minimized I have this damn file-manager thingy on the bar that I can't do anything with [Screenshot](http://home.comcast.net/~seeker5528/Screenshot-Gnome3.png), in my Ubuntu install it shows as x-nautilus-desktop [Screenshot](http://home.comcast.net/~seeker5528/Screenshot-Gnome3-3.png) but other than that, same thing.

Later, Seeker

### Post by OlyPerson on 2010-02-02
> **NightwishFan said:**
> Fantastic. The new gnome shell is worth it. I love the notifications, though I had to disable a gconf setting in Empathy for it to show up in my panel.

I agree, definitely worth it.

One thing I was wondering other people's opinions on was something I was thinking about the other day-
Ubuntu tries to be competitive with other modern OSs yet still strives to be usable on older computers- the other operating systems (read Windows and OSX) do not have this issue.  At what point should Ubuntu abandon deprecated hardware in favor of what is newer in order to be more competitive?  Is this what we're starting to see with GS?

And yes I realize GS isn't Ubuntu specific, but I think if Ubuntu adopts this new tech it's a step forward with competing.  And I do realize there is compiz but that really isn't quite the same.  Also, gnome-panels (and x,k,flux, etcbuntu) will still be available to people whose computers can't run GS.

### Post by Roasted on 2010-02-02
> **Merk42 said:**
> I'm pretty sure it's impossible by design.
I see your post on the mailing list went the one of two ways a suggestion goes "completely ignored" (the other being argued against because it doesn't fit their "vision")

Their vision? That's ********. Their vision is to make Gnome Shell user friendly and easy to use. Right now there's no logical way to switch through active applications without the use of Alt Tab and the overlay mode. There needs to be something more or this thing won't get mass used as they want it.

Come on, developers.

EDIT - This calls for another email to the mailing list.

---

### Post by Keyper7 on 2010-02-02
> **Roasted said:**
> Their vision? That's ********. Their vision is to make Gnome Shell user friendly and easy to use. Right now there's no logical way to switch through active applications without the use of Alt Tab and the overlay mode. There needs to be something more or this thing won't get mass used as they want it.

The problem with your posts is that you present your vision with adjectives like "logical" and "easier" and more or less ignores the fact that it's a matter of opinion. Thus, you indirectly insult those who do not agree.

Ever since I started using the Compiz scale/expo plugins with hot corners, I lost all need for a taskbar and my usage is very mouse-centric. The gnome-shell approach is very close to how I use my computer right now and I don't feel it's keyboard centric at all.

For me, it's a matter of opinion. You have every right to be unconfortable with Gnome-shell. But when I read your recent posts, the impression I have is that I don't have the right to feel confortable. Both in here and the mailing lists, the impression I have is that you are calling me an idiot who likes the "logically harder" approach. I know you don't have such intention, but impression counts.

My suggestion is that you should start to list solid technical reasons for a taskbar or at least convincing arguments about how this decision is controversial enough that the best approach is to offer both choices. Remember: the Gnome philosophy is that "because you can" is not a good reason to customize. All customization options should be throughly thought to see if they are worthy the extra complexity they introduce. And "because that's what people are used to" is not a good reason either, because if we were to always follow that logic I'd never have tried multiple desktops when coming from Windows.

Also remember, [Linux is NOT about choice](https://www.redhat.com/archives/fedora-devel-list/2008-January/msg00861.html).

---

I apologize that you got the completely wrong impression from my posts. That was not my intention and I'm not really sure how you dug that up, but it is what it is.

I was presenting my "logical" standpoint from the simple fact that a huge number of Gnome Shell users out there are on the same page I am. I'm just the guy who's not afraid to present it in a demeanor to get the developers to actually look at my suggestion.

I've made several suggestions, the initial ones coming off with a tone that was "Hey I really like your work guys, if at all possible perhaps this would be an idea worth considering..." etc. But what happened? It got rejected. Tossed under the desk. Whatever. Didn't care. How's that for an open source attitude? "Hey guys, email us your suggestions!" OH, wait, yeah, we already have a game plan laid out so yeah let's just ignore these incoming posts.

I'm sick of seeing all of these users on the same page and the developers simply saying "No." For something as big as Gnome, it's not something I find acceptable.

Bottom line is this. If you like the way it's laid out now, that's great. I have said time and time again, it's not that I'm saying this is how it HAS to be and that's it - what I've presented every single time was there is a problem. The problem is simple. A lot of users out there are not liking the lack of an easy way to switch between active windows. Some users like Alt-Tab. Some users like overlay. Great. Use what works for you. THAT'S what I've said every single time I've presented my ideas - mailing list included. And because of that, I find it to be such a stretch to assume that I was indirectly calling you a moron.

Despite all of this, my opinion still stands. I believe there needs to be some sort of option for the users out there who want to have some sort of easier (and on screen) way to navigate between windows, besides Alt+Tab and besides the overlay.

If you want to assume I'm still insulting you, fine. I'm not going to back down from my opinion because somebody is taking the words I say in the opposite manner they were intended for.

---

### Post by gsmanners on 2010-02-02
So, by design it's impossible to switch tasks with one mouse click? Is this true?

If that is the case, then Gnome Shell will not be received well.

### Post by Merk42 on 2010-02-02
> **gsmanners said:**

### Post by Roasted on 2010-02-02
> **gsmanners said:**
> So, by design it's impossible to switch tasks with one mouse click? Is this true?

If that is the case, then Gnome Shell will not be received well.

I guess it depends. "Technically" it's one mouse click, because when you hover the mouse over the Activities menu, it does go into overlay mode, and from there you can single click to bring up the corresponding window as the primary one.

Search on YouTube for videos on Gnome Shell if you haven't installed it already to get a better feel of how it works. I love the overlay and I love what it's capable of. I also like how I can simply hover over each window and use the scroll wheel to zoom in/out of only that window. It's an interface that is very slick.

But in essence, there is no on-screen way to switch between applications without going into overlay mode or utilizing the alt-tab feature on the keyboard. 

As some users have said, they're fine with it as is. But I feel like there needs to be an additional option to help out for those of us who want something more. I suppose we could use docks. I just hate feeling that way though... knowing there's no integrated method to handle that task. It makes GS look like an unfinished product that requires that last bit of duct tape to hold together. Granted, I suppose it is since it's barely even in alpha stage yet... but I'm speaking from the standpoint of "if it were released tomorrow..."

Again, just my **opinion.**

---

### Post by Roasted on 2010-02-02
> **Merk42 said:**
> Currently yes, though I'm sure some will be pedantic and say "no" as going to the overlay doesn't require a click.

I guess it depends on your setup, as well.

At home I use dual screen monitors, with my secondary monitor on the left, which means the "Activities" button is along the border/corner of my primary monitor. My mouse can continue traveling beyond the hot corner, which makes it pretty much impossible to activate the overlay WITHOUT clicking.

On my laptop, it's a different story, since my mouse will hit the corner and stop, activating the hot corner accordingly. But like I said, my desktop isn't in the same boat. The mouse will just skim right past the sweet spot to activate the overlay, making clicking (in this particular instance) pretty much a requirement.

---

It's like people who flat-out say that the default Ubuntu theme is ugly and "should" be changed: so people who like it have poor taste and like ugly things?

> **Roasted said:**
> I was presenting my "logical" standpoint from the simple fact that a huge number of Gnome Shell users out there are on the same page I am.

And I'm willing to bet a huge number of users are not. And also, that a huge number of users would like Hannah Montana as the default wallpaper.

For this argument to be relevant, there is the need of either (a) reliable statistics showing that the number of unhappy users is significant (statistics taken from a specific thread of a specific section of a specific board of a specific distro is hardly reliable) or (b) more solid foundation such as detailed usability studies and experience reports (obs: "I didn't like it because it's different than what I used to" is not a valid experience report)

> **Roasted said:**
> I'm just the guy who's not afraid to present it in a demeanor to get the developers to actually look at my suggestion.

And my point is that your demeanor does **not** make developers look at your suggestion. It only makes them take you less seriously, as someone who claims something is "better" without technical justification.

> **Roasted said:**
> I've made several suggestions, the initial ones coming off with a tone that was "Hey I really like your work guys, if at all possible perhaps this would be an idea worth considering..." etc. But what happened? It got rejected. Tossed under the desk. Whatever. Didn't care. How's that for an open source attitude? "Hey guys, email us your suggestions!" OH, wait, yeah, we already have a game plan laid out so yeah let's just ignore these incoming posts. I'm sick of seeing all of these users on the same page and the developers simply saying "No." For something as big as Gnome, it's not something I find acceptable.

You are confusing politeness with objectiveness. The first is important and I applaud you for not forgetting it, but the second is essential. I didn't see all of your posts, but the ones I saw had no convincing arguments whatsoever, only "it's better", "it's easier", "there exists people who agree with me".

In my opinion, the one who is simply saying "no" is you.

> **Roasted said:**
> Bottom line is this. If you like the way it's laid out now, that's great. I have said time and time again, it's not that I'm saying this is how it HAS to be and that's it - what I've presented every single time was there is a problem. The problem is simple. A lot of users out there are not liking the lack of an **easy** way to switch between active windows. Some users like Alt-Tab. Some users like overlay. Great. Use what works for you. THAT'S what I've said every single time I've presented my ideas - mailing list included. And because of that, I find it to be such a stretch to assume that I was indirectly calling you a moron.

Despite all of this, my opinion still stands. I believe there needs to be some sort of option for the users out there who want to have some sort of **easier** (and on screen) way to navigate between windows, besides Alt+Tab and besides the overlay.

**(emphasis mine)**

Here you go again. So the current ways are **not** easy?

Saying "use whatever works for you" is useless if right after you say "but we need a better way".

> **Roasted said:**

I never assumed that, as it's very clear in my previous post.

Please notice that I'm not saying you are wrong in wanting changes, or asking for them. I'm trying to help you to present a better case and be better heard in the list. Please do not take my words in the wrong way, I'm often guilty of wrong tone myself. :)

### Post by gsmanners on 2010-02-02

I wonder if it's possible to keep the "overlay" visible all the time rather than hidden by default. Maybe with a more compact design that wouldn't use as much screen real estate. Or has this all been "yes, but"-ed to death already?

I can also change the theme if I don't like it. Because I have that **option.**

> 
And I'm willing to bet a huge number of users are not. And also, that a huge number of users would like Hannah Montana as the default wallpaper.


Again, I can still change the wallpaper. I have the ability to do that.

> 
For this argument to be relevant, there is the need of either (a) reliable statistics showing that the number of unhappy users is significant (statistics taken from a specific thread of a specific section of a specific board of a specific distro is hardly reliable) or (b) more solid foundation such as detailed usability studies and experience reports (obs: "I didn't like it because it's different than what I used to" is not a valid experience report)


Well, I didn't exactly take a survey. It's just based on common talk that I've shared here with other users, along with Ubuntu-using friends/family in real life as well as the Ubuntu and Gnome IRC channel. There are only 2 people who outright told me "I like Gnome Shell as is and would use it in a heartbeat. It needs nothing else." Everyone else argued (if they voiced an opinion) that they wanted a way to see what applications are actively running on the screen. If this option was not implemented, they would either:

A - stick with 2.X until it dies.
B - go to KDE or XFCE.
C - find a fork of Gnome that would still utilize the 2.X style panels.
D - simply use a dock.

I did not do an official survey, no. But I'm a big boy now. I can count beyond 2... and I can assure you the users who were comfortable with it as-is was not exceeding 2. But hey, it's still in development. Who knows what'll happen tomorrow/next week/next month, etc.

> 
And my point is that your demeanor does **not** make developers look at your suggestion. It only makes them take you less seriously, as someone who claims something is "better" without technical justification.


I don't know what official things I need to show developers it's something that's worth integrating. It's an option that a lot of users want to see. A lot of users are saying if they DON'T see it, they won't use Gnome Shell and would simply jump ship to another interface. A lot of them are saying they would just use a dock to fill in the void Gnome Shell leaves open.

I've spoken to developers. Nobody listened. If my demeanor is, what you call, unprofessional - so be it. I'm voicing my opinion. I've tried the nice route, and I was ignored. I want the developers to consider this suggestion, or else I feel like a lot of hardcore current Gnome fans will be rather upset come d-day.

> 
You are confusing politeness with objectiveness. The first is important and I applaud you for not forgetting it, but the second is essential. I didn't see all of your posts, but the ones I saw had no convincing arguments whatsoever, only "it's better", "it's easier", "there exists people who agree with me".

In my opinion, the one who is simply saying "no" is you.


If only I didn't delete my former emails I received from the mailing list, I could show you exactly where certain developers told me that feature would not get implemented. The ironic part is, it ended up getting implemented anyway. There were 3 or 4 things I suggested to the GS mailing team, and they simply said no, it won't happen, this is why, it doesn't fit with what we're trying to do, etc. So I bailed. I went to KDE. After KDE drove me crazy I figured I'd suck it up and force myself to like GS. Guess what? Grabbed the updates for GS, fired it up - well look at that. The very same features I suggested are implemented. Because of me? Oh gosh, I doubt it. I would only assume enough other users voiced their opinion while I was off in KDE land to make it a worthy adjustment in the eyes of the developers.

I really don't know what kind of justification is needed for some sort of dock-like or ubuntu netbook remix-like task manager to be implemented. It's just easier. It's my opinion. I'm not calling you stupid and I'm not calling anybody else stupid. I'm not insulting anybody whatsoever. I'm telling you, as a user of Gnome Shell, I feel as though it is lacking in "XYZ" department for "ABC" reasons. Period. That's where I was saying it was easier, it needs to have a more logical way to manage tasks - and I'm saying that because I firmly believe it. It's an opinion. Not an insult. There's a difference.

I understand the overlay, and quite honestly - I love it. I even used an overlay-like feature in KDE. But I used the overlay in tandem with the panel. It was so convenient because I could mix and match how I went about my business on my computer. Having to zoom in/out whenever I want to switch from pidgin to firefox to thunderbird to exaile to pidgin again back to firefox over to remote desktop viewer and on to open office, etc. When you're a huge multitasker and you're bouncing through at on of windows, the zooming in/out of the overlay constantly can be sickening. I love it. But I like utilizing the overlay for specific things - not for "everything." And yeah, alt-tab is there too. It's nice when I'm bouncing between pidgin/firefox over and over, but to utilize it as my main way to sift through applications? It aint working. At least for me, it aint working.

> 
Here you go again. So the current ways are **not** easy?

Saying "use whatever works for you" is useless if right after you say "but we need a better way".


No, it's not useless. You're happy with using the overlay to switch through active tasks, correct? Okay, good. The conversation does not need to go further because **you** are happy with how Gnome Shell works. That's what matters to you. That's where the debate ends. I however am not happy with the way I'm forced to use it to manage open tasks. But it's such a crapper because the user interface is so genius and so cleanly laid out. I'm not asking for a complete overhaul to cater to MY needs. I just think it'd be a nice feature. An option. Something to go into the control panel and enable. For example, take Windows XP. You have to enable the quick launch in order to put icons in your taskbar. I LOVE that. It's genius! I use it all the time at work. Why can't we have something like that for the relatively large user base who supports it and wants it? 

It's important to realize that everybody is different, and everybody will have different needs and desires when using a computer. That's what makes Linux so great because there's so much we can do with it. Don't like a panel? Kill it. Want a dock? Download it. Want two docks? Docky2 can handle that. Want no panels and just a dock to handle everything? That's an option too. Want icons in the top panel to quick launch items? Yep, it's possible. The list goes on and on and on. 

Maybe I'm just having a severe mental block here or something, but I just honestly do not see what is so bad about adding additional features (and a minor one at that) that a large amount of GS users would probably like. I'm trying to do my best as a faithful user of Ubuntu/Gnome to voice my opinion because I am convinced that somebody other than myself will enable that feature (if it gets implemented) and be like, aww yeah! I get this! I like it! 

I'd really like to see some sort of an option available, because I know there will be other users out there who will utilize it.

### Post by Roasted on 2010-02-03
I'd like to post something I recently encountered. I ended up speaking to several of the Gnome Shell developers, who were nothing short of awesome to talk to. While it is apparent that they have a vision and a specific way they want to see Gnome Shell implemented, there's a sense of flexibility I felt when I was talking with them. There's a lot of ideas floating on the table... ideas that just might address the several dislikes other users might have in regard to certain things about Gnome Shell... including mine, in regard to my feelings about the task management system. 

The bottom line is, they hear us loud and clear, and are taking baby steps with implementing different ideas to see what the reaction of the users are. They encourage heavy usage of Gnome Shell and constructive feedback because at the end of the day, it's only going to help.

I just wanted to share my 2 cents on what I recently experienced. I have no doubt now that Gnome Shell will land with a decent following of users. The optimism and the "well, let's try this first and see how it goes, then if not we'll figure out an alternative" approach definitely sounds promising.

Let's see what happens tomorrow/next week/month... :popcorn:

Will I still be able to use Metacity and the panel with Gnome 3?

Then the answer is yes. I don't know how long it will be maintained as an official part of Gnome, but I expect it will be around for some time to come. Sawfish hasn't been part of Gnome for quite some time, but it is still maintained, and you can still use it as your window manager in Gnome.

As far as maintaining compatibility with older machines is concerned, I started using LXDE on one of my machines and I kind of like it for that. A little bit different of a menu structure for the applications, but it's organized in a reasonably sane way.

### Post by Reiger on 2010-02-05
> **xtoudi said:**
> ok. We've got 'Add to favorites' in 'Activities' but:
using this I only can add some app which is already started. What if I need set something before?? I've got a scripts witch at the end starts main program - and I need start this script (not only a program). How can I add this script to 'Favorites'??? Adding already started program results adding a main program (no a script).

EDIT:
in detail: I mean some java program, script looks like this:

java -jar -Xmx512m ~/../../JDownloader/JDownloader.jar

this runs main java jar. And of course I cannot add this to Favorites using 'Add to favorites'. This does not work - after that GS not know how to run this again. So I need to add this mannualy by editing some file (I think) or in some other way - but how?

You can make a .desktop file for it in your ~/.local/ tree I expect. Alternatively you could put it under /usr/local/share/applications/.

The addition of the .desktop file should make the menu system pick up a new executable command (when you click it, the Exec line will be run; similar to a Windows .lnk file). Just take an example .desktop file from /usr/share/applications/ as template, modify to meet your need. Make sure the .desktop file you save is executable; make sure the files you refer to (the jar) are executable as well.

### Post by OlyPerson on 2010-02-06
> **xtoudi said:**
> OK .... so how can I make anything transparent?? Without extensions if it's easier.

If you didn't build it from source go to /usr/share/gnome-shell/theme/gnome-shell.css and you can change what you what to be transparent.  Here's an example of how to change the panel:
Originally:
```
#panel {
    color: #ffffff;
    font-size: 16px;
    background-gradient-direction: vertical;
    background-gradient-start: #161616;
    background-gradient-end: #000000;
}
```

Change to:
```
#panel {
    color: #ffffff;
    font-size: 16px;
    background-color: rgba(0,0,0,0.75);
}
```

The rgba can handle transparency.  Note that every time GS updates it will be erased.

While I am surprised at how low of resources Gnome Shell requires to run nicely, I still call BS on the fact it can be ran on "any system that can already handle 2.X fine."

### Post by autocrosser on 2010-02-07
If you are building from source or have a real current build you got a big mesa change.....```
Use GL_MESA_pack_invert if available
Use GL_MESA_pack_invert if available to avoid doing flipping in software.
https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=609053 

```that speeds things up a bit.....

### Post by k3lt01 on 2010-02-08
I don't know if this has already been covered or not, and atm I really don't feel like reading 91 pages to see if it has ;), but I think they need to do more work on the application selector. If someone new comes to Ubuntu, or any version of Linux using G-S as the default, how will they know what programs are available on their system (i.e. already installed)? 

There is the favourite list and the scroll list but they don't include everything. So in the future if someone new starts using Ubuntu with G-S they may think Ubuntu doesn't have many programs installed and this would be incorrect simply because G-S hasn't listed them at all and the new user may not know what they are called so they wont know what to search for.

---

### Post by pomnikus on 2010-02-09
The one of the most horrible thing in GS for me is their redesigned Alt+Tab. It's an app switcher now, not proper Alt+Tab which is used for quick switching between apps. The proper behaviour is: Alt+Tab switch an app at once. The GS Alt+Tab activates Alt+Tab overlay but keeps current app. It's highly unlikely they will change this behaviour.

### Post by phenest on 2010-02-09

That's what I said, except you've over-emphasized on it. The keyboard can, and should, be used to navigate your desktop and application rather than rely solely on the mouse. There are actions that can be performed far quicker via the keyboard than using the mouse, and vice versa. There are plenty of actions I do via the mouse, but I don't have my hand next to the mouse at all. It's about utilizing a piece of hardware that is largely unused unless you're typing a letter, and has between 80 and 100 keys (or more). How many more actions can be performed by the keyboard than the mouse? And they're all direct actions.

> **k3lt01 said:**
> Phenest you totally missed the point.

The only point I will make is that GS is 7/8 months away from release and it's not even alpha. If you feel it needs more options/features then tell the Gnome devs rather than hope they'll put it in. I'd at least wait until it's alpha/beta before I start complaining.

### Post by Roasted on 2010-02-09
> **phenest said:**
> That's what I said, except you've over-emphasized on it. The keyboard can, and should, be used to navigate your desktop and application rather than rely solely on the mouse. There are actions that can be performed far quicker via the keyboard than using the mouse, and vice versa. There are plenty of actions I do via the mouse, but I don't have my hand next to the mouse at all. It's about utilizing a piece of hardware that is largely unused unless you're typing a letter, and has between 80 and 100 keys (or more). How many more actions can be performed by the keyboard than the mouse? And they're all direct actions.



The only point I will make is that GS is 7/8 months away from release and it's not even alpha. If you feel it needs more options/features then tell the Gnome devs rather than hope they'll put it in. Try and see what it is they're trying to develop here, instead of whine like it's a finished product. I can see the potential of GS, and the devs aim. I'd at least wait until it's alpha/beta before I start complaining.

While I do agree, I get frustrated on this subject because I HAVE spoken directly to the developers. And while they do seem to be like very nice people who are relatively open minded, I get this underlying notion that they won't ever integrate something like that simply because it doesn't integrate with their vision. Let's put the BS aside and be realistic... they're designing a user interface for people to use that will in time replace Gnome 2.X. There is no "let's try something completely new" option here. If you keep 2.X and want to throw out a spinner option, that's great. But when you're redesigning a new-age user interface, you need to integrate options for the users to utilize it at its maximum. Otherwise, people simply won't use it, which is unfortunately a large part of the current consensus SIMPLY based on the lack of a window/app switcher. 

However, you make a valid point. It's not even alpha. And that's very true. Once beta hits, the exposure GS will get will be exponentially higher than what it is now - only then will it be even more clearer to the Gnome devs if they should put their one-laned vision aside and integrate something that the masses want. 

But the bottom line is, GS is absolutely fantastic and very nice to use. It still has a ways to go, but considering it's not even in Alpha, it's pretty damn commendable to the devs to see what they have so far.

### Post by NightwishFan on 2010-02-09
I only know of one..

Window Attention Extension:
[http://cloud.github.com/downloads/linux4kix/gnome-shell-extensions/window_attention_extension.tar.gz](http://cloud.github.com/downloads/linux4kix/gnome-shell-extensions/window_attention_extension.tar.gz)

Extract it into ~/.config/gnome-shell/extensions, and restart the shell. They say you need a newer shell, not sure what that qualifies as. You can restart a running shell by typing ALT+f2 and typing "r".

NOTE: I did not test this personally.

### Post by Roasted on 2010-02-09
> **phenest said:**
> I'm glad you do talk to them. I, for one, have found them to be rude which is off-putting. I have also read reports of users asking the devs to implement/change a feature only to be refused, yet later on, they have done what was asked albeit seemingly 'off their own back'. Make of that what you will, but they do appear visionary, inventive, almost ingenious, and too many put them down for being simplistic and/or arrogant.

I apologise now if I appear argumentative, but I like to see users making full use of their machines and their software, and I dislike it when users seem too fixed in their ways. But I guess taste cannot always be accounted for.

And that is completely understandable. But I see no harm in adding -options- for users to make their desktop as efficient as possible for them.

It all falls back to the basic principle of customizing your computer. I run the Moomex theme. Do you run the Moomex theme? If not, why not? I run it. Why don't you? It's all a matter of preference, and how users utilize their desktop is yet another chapter in this ongoing story with customizing.

Take Windows XP for example. It has a quick launch. I love it. But it's not enabled by default. No big deal, I can enable it. A simple checkbox allows me as a user to enjoy my computing experience that much more. Using that example against GS begs the obvious question - why is such a small feature that some users absolutely would love such a bad thing to integrate as an option they may enable? 

Falling back to a similar swing on the topic, if they don't integrate some sort of window/app switcher, I would like the option to turn OFF the notification system in the bottom of the screen. Why? Because if GS won't integrate an option to handle that task, I'll be using Docky to fill in the gap. Problem is, I don't want the auto hide notification system to be in the way, which is why I'd want an option to turn it off.

And of course, if neither of those things happen, there's only one thing I can say: 2.X for life!

---

### Post by Kenny_Strawn on 2010-02-09
> **Roasted said:**
> 
Falling back to a similar swing on the topic, [color="Red"]if they don't integrate some sort of window/app switcher[/color], I would like the option to turn OFF the notification system in the bottom of the screen. Why? Because if GS won't integrate an option to handle that task, I'll be using Docky to fill in the gap. Problem is, I don't want the auto hide notification system to be in the way, which is why I'd want an option to turn it off.

And of course, if neither of those things happen, there's only one thing I can say: 2.X for life!

Actually, there are two ways of going about this: the Overview, or Alt+Tab. Personally, if you don't like Alt+Tab, just move your mouse into the upper left-hand corner of the screen. This activate the Hot Corner, which activates the Overview. You can then see all your windows laid out in an OS X Expose-like tiled window layout.

---

### Post by Roasted on 2010-02-09
> **Kenny_Strawn said:**
> Actually, there are two ways of going about this: the Overview, or Alt+Tab. Personally, if you don't like Alt+Tab, just move your mouse into the upper left-hand corner of the screen. This activate the Hot Corner, which activates the Overview. You can then see all your windows laid out in an OS X Expose-like tiled window layout.

I know. The Alt+Tab thing is not a way a lot of users like to switch applications. I for one LOVE Alt+Tab, but not for managing all of my open applications. If I'm going between two IMs, I love Alt+Tab then because I can real quick snap those keys together and blam, I'm on the other window.

I've tried to use Alt+Tab as my main way of switching through open applications. I'm sorry, it's very convenient, but it is in no way shape or form efficient enough to be the main way I switch through all active applications.

The overview is nice, but again, it comes with limitations. The overview in my mind (strictly my opinion here) should be a way to manage windows in workspaces. When you begin to use the overview for all means of switching through active applications, it can become a little blah.

Firefox. Overview. Thunderbird. Overview. Pidgin. Overview. Audacious. Overview. Firefox again. Overview. 

I spent a month trying to force myself to use that on my work laptop, since GS is unusually stable despite its infancy stage. However, I just couldn't get into it. Each time I felt myself firing up Docky and breathing a sigh of relief.

Through talking with other users, through the forums, through showing friends, family members, asking on the Ubuntu and Gnome IRC chat, etc, the common consensus is that people love the overview, alt+tab is always a nice feature to have, but it's still lacking that main on-screen way to switch apps.

There are a few users out there who love the way GS is set up right now. Out of the three to four dozen people I showed GS to, I can count on 1 hand how many agree that the way it is right now, today, is perfect in regard to application management.

But oh well, I've gotten over my stance of being pissy about it. I've voiced my opinion on the matter and think it's best for now to be patient and at least wait until beta before I begin bugging the developers again on this issue. I just simply won't use Gnome Shell if some sort of other way is implemented that makes a little more sense for us users out there who aren't a fan of the other methods in place currently.

---

### Post by Merk42 on 2010-02-09
> **Kenny_Strawn said:**
> Actually, there are two ways of going about this: the Overview, or Alt+Tab. Personally, if you don't like Alt+Tab, just move your mouse into the upper left-hand corner of the screen. This activate the Hot Corner, which activates the Overview. You can then see all your windows laid out in an OS X Expose-like tiled window layout.

Roasted, and pretty much everyone else asking for a better app switcher, know this.

That way is very much inferior to the 2.X way:
[list][*]it requires more mouse movement
[*]you have to move the mouse first then look (since opening closing applications affects position so there is no muscle memory)
[*]it doesn't have an infinite height.[/list]

### Post by Roasted on 2010-02-10
> **Kenny_Strawn said:**
> After you move the mouse into the hot corner, it's only one click (on the window that you want).

Let's add a curve ball: I run dual monitors. My 2nd monitor is on the left. That means I don't have a "hot corner" since there is no corner. If I move to the activities button, my cursor keeps continuing. I have to make it a point to actually navigate to that button and click. 

In short: The overview is awesome. But it is not that efficient for switching windows/apps. Some may think so, but for me and many others, there needs to be something else.

### Post by xtoudi on 2010-02-10
> **tito_torrisi said:**
> Thank you! I have the ricotz testing ppa version, most recent one from yesterday! I hope that will also work.

And if you want exactly top panel like at this screen you need to set:

```
#panel {
    color: #ffffff;
    font-size: 16px;
    background-gradient-direction: vertical;
    background-color: rgba(0,0,0,0.98);
}
```

### Post by Roasted on 2010-02-10
> **Kenny_Strawn said:**
> You should always run into the habit of putting your desktop on the monitor on the left, especially with GS.

I don't mean to sound like an ***, I truly don't, but I have a blunt answer.

My desk layout doesn't allow for me to put my monitor on the right, due to the unique L-shaped layout it is made out of. I don't see at all how it's a valid argument to say "Oh yeah, GS... you running dual monitors? Ahh, man.. gotta have the secondary on your right. Sorry bro."

And to even further add, I run decently large monitors. For me to have to cross both monitors and hot corner, that's not as user friendly as hitting the bottom of my main screen for a dock or a panel. Point being, even if I had all of the desk space in the world and could put my second monitor up down left right upside down or any which direction to accommodate to Gnome Shell's preferences, I STILL would find that claim to be poor.

### Post by gsmanners on 2010-02-11
Reading the Usability Testing page, it looks like reducing distractions is a high priority.

[http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell/Design/UsabilityTesting/PhaseI](http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell/Design/UsabilityTesting/PhaseI)

I counted 5 assumptions from their list that are based on the notion that reducing distractions increases usability. I gather that this is a high priority for the GS team, and I wonder if they really feel that this notion is even worth testing. I just mention this because I looked over the proposed testing methodology, and it seemed to me like they were confusing task performance with usability.

### Post by xtoudi on 2010-02-11
If someone want to get rid **'RECENT ITEMS'** (Overview) need to change **dash.js** file (if you have git version file should be here: ~/gnome-shell/source/gnome-shell/js/ui)

and we need delete this:

```
        /***** Documents *****/

        this._docsSection = new Section(_("RECENT ITEMS"));

        this._docDisplay = new DocDisplay.DashDocDisplay();
        this._docsSection.content.add(this._docDisplay.actor, { expand: true });

        this._moreDocsPane = null;
        this._docsSection.header.moreLink.connect('activated', Lang.bind(this, function (link) {
            if (this._moreDocsPane == null) {
                this._moreDocsPane = new ResultPane(this);
                this._moreDocsPane.packResults(DOCS);
                this._addPane(this._moreDocsPane);
                link.setPane(this._moreDocsPane);
           }
        }));

        this._docDisplay.connect('changed', Lang.bind(this, function () {
            this._docsSection.header.setMoreLinkVisible(
                this._docDisplay.actor.get_children().length > 0);
        }));
        this._docDisplay.emit('changed');

        this.sectionArea.add(this._docsSection.actor, { expand: true });
```

After that just Alt+F2 and type 'r' <enter>.

And there is no such ugly thing like 'RECENT ITEMS' in overview :-)

*ugly thing = only my opinion*

EDIT:
After that searching, rising ... seems to be **faster**! At least first run overview.

### Post by Roasted on 2010-02-11

### Post by 23meg on 2010-02-11
> **Roasted said:**
> 
Also - why plan on switching at all? From what I've read, I haven't seen anything that indicates they will kill off Gnome panels (2.X) at all. I think it'll just turn into an option...

I'd like to emphasize this point: if at present you are a happy user of GNOME 2.x, don't like where GNOME Shell is heading for one reason or another, and have no intention to take an active role in shaping it, no matter how small, **you don't have to switch to anything**.

GNOME Panel and Metacity, which are being replaced by GNOME Shell, as well as Compiz, which you may prefer, will still be maintained and usable, and you'll be able to continue using them along with the other benefits of GNOME 3 such as a more modern and consolidated platform, GNOME Activity Journal, and all the latest GNOME-related technologies and software.

### Post by Psumi on 2010-02-13
> **Tompalaz said:**
> Is it mutter that will replace metacity?
If it is, is there a plan/roadmap when this will happen?

Excited what will happen with GS :popcorn:

No.

Mutter = GTK2 Window Manager (Compositor)
Metacity = Decorator

This will be the case with GNOME Shell.

### Post by seeker5528 on 2010-02-13
> **JCoster said:**
> Anyone else with this problem (totally up-to-date using radeon driver):

The radeon driver from the main repository works fine for me.

This makes it look like something is not right with your mesa/drm stuff.

> glxinfo: error while loading shared libraries: libGL.so.1: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory

Doing```
dpkg -l *drm* *mesa* | grep ii
```

shows these being installed on my system

```

ii  libdrm-intel1                2.4.17-0ubuntu2
ii  libdrm-nouveau1              2.4.17-0ubuntu2
ii  libdrm-radeon1               2.4.17-0ubuntu2
ii  libdrm2                      2.4.17-0ubuntu2
ii  libgl1-mesa-dev              7.7-0ubuntu8
ii  libgl1-mesa-dri              7.7-0ubuntu8
ii  libgl1-mesa-glx              7.7-0ubuntu8
ii  libglu1-mesa                 7.7-0ubuntu8
ii  libglu1-mesa-dev             7.7-0ubuntu8
ii  mesa-common-dev              7.7-0ubuntu8
ii  mesa-utils                   7.7-0ubuntu8
ii  xlibmesa-gl-dev              1:7.5+1ubuntu2
ii  xserver-xorg-video-radeon    1:6.12.99+git20100126.e5933fd7-0ubuntu1
ii  xserver-xorg-video-radeonhd  1.3.0-2

```

glxinfo should report something similar to

```
seeker@ubuntu:~$ glxinfo
name of display: :0.0
display: :0  screen: 0
direct rendering: Yes
server glx vendor string: SGI
server glx version string: 1.4
```

and xdriinfo something like:

```
seeker@ubuntu:~$ xdriinfo 
Screen 0: r300
```

The libgl1-mesa-glx is the one that supplies libGL.so.1 so if all the above packages look like they are in installed on your system you might try reinstalling that one package or possibly all of them just to be sure:

```
sudo apt-get install --reinstall libgl1-mesa-glx
```

Later, Seeker

---

### Post by JCoster on 2010-02-14
> **seeker5528 said:**
> The libgl1-mesa-glx is the one that supplies libGL.so.1 so if all the above packages look like they are in installed on your system you might try reinstalling that one package or possibly all of them just to be sure:

```
sudo apt-get install --reinstall libgl1-mesa-glx
```

Awesome, cheers!  That solved it for me.

Thanks a lot

### Post by shafin on 2010-02-15
> **moore.bryan said:**
> Has anyone been able/figured-out how to change the clock formatting?

Edit /usr/share/gnome-shell/js/ui/panel.js
See lines 557 to 564
```
     let isTime24h = displayDate.toLocaleFormat("x%p") == "x";
    if  (isTime24h) {
      /* Translators: This is the time format used in  24-hour mode. */
       this._clock.set_text(displayDate.toLocaleFormat(_("%a %R")));
    }  else {
      /* Translators: This is a time format used for AM/PM. */
       this._clock.set_text(displayDate.toLocaleFormat(_("%a %l:%M %p")));
     }
```
This is where clock formatting is set.

       this._clock.set_text(displayDate.toLocaleFormat(_("%a %R")));
    }  else {
      /* Translators: This is a time format used for AM/PM. */
       this._clock.set_text(displayDate.toLocaleFormat(_("%a %l:%M %p")));
     }
```
This is where clock formatting is set.
I've done this before, but it didn't change. Now, it would seem my GnomeShell is borked because it won't start and complains:
```

 Window manager warning: Log level 16: Could not load library [/home/bryan/gnome-shell/install/lib/mutter/plugins/libgnome-shell.so (libmozjs.so: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory)]
Window manager warning: Log level 8: failed to load plugins

```
Nevermind about the error; somehow, xulrunner was uninstalled. I reinstalled and everything's fine except for the time/date... still not right, even though I have the following in panel.js:
```

	/* If there is no am or pm, time format is 24h */
	let isTime24h = displayDate.toLocaleFormat("x%p") == "x";
	if (isTime24h) {
	  /* Translators: This is the time format used in 24-hour mode. */
	  this._clock.set_text(displayDate.toLocaleFormat(_("%a %R")));
	} else {
	  /* Translators: This is a time format used for AM/PM. */
	  this._clock.set_text(displayDate.toLocaleFormat(_("%A, %d %B %Y | %l:%M %p")));
	}
        Mainloop.timeout_add(msecRemaining, Lang.bind(this, this._updateClock));
        return false;
    },

```

EDIT:
I solved this by just installing from the PPA repo rather than building myself through git and making the changes above.

I have to say, I like GnomeShell so much better than I thought I would...

### Post by Tompalaz on 2010-02-20
How do i change a icon in gnome-shell and docky?
I use geany but the icon is truly awfull, i'd like to change it.

I've edit /usr/share/menu/geany
replaced a icon in /usr/share/pixmaps etc...

---

### Post by techno-mole on 2010-02-20
you can right click on the top right corner (where you user name is if your using gnome shell) from there click system preferences and look for "Main Menu", then look for the application you want to edit (eg - internet >> firefox) and then select it and on the right you should see some options, look for the one that says "Properties"

 you should then see an icon on the left of the window, click it, you can select an image from there, or if you want to select another image click browse and go to the location of the image you want, then click open.
you should then be able to select the icon you want.

to get the icon to show up in gnome shell press "alt and f2" and type "restart" it should work, ive just changed the icon for firefox this way.

editing things like this is a small issue in gnome shell, for example to change the colour of the panel and the overview require editing a css file, which for those that know css isnt too much trouble, but for less experienced users the ability to change themes,colours etc easily will be a feature that (in my opinion) will be needed.

i have been looking at the various files that control the colours of the panel etc, i have also been looking at editing the various svg files that are used for the various indicators (for example the ripple effect that shows in the top left when you move the mouse to that corner)

i have wondered whether theme packs could be built with the various files needed to change the colours etc, and also containing the various svg icons that are used in different colours and such like, of course they might need an easier way to install them, although moving the folder that contains the theme to the right place isnt too hard.

### Post by techno-mole on 2010-02-20
from what i know gnome shell is a new window manager, like metacity compiz etc, only it doesnt use compositing (so you dont need compiz running to get it to work, in fact running gnome shell kills compiz)

to be honest i dont know that much about it, but from what ive seen (im using it as well) it is a good idea (in my opinion) some people have said its a bad idea, but i dont think it is.

you should probably have a look here - [http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell](http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell)

as for the menu thing, im not sure how it works, but i assume that when gnome shell is running when editing menus the way i posted, i assume gnome shell interfaces with alacarte (which is the gnome menu editor - [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alacarte](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alacarte))

you can also run alacarte in a terminal and it will also bring up the menu editor, i had a small issue with alacarte when i first installed karmic, it would only let me edit menus if i was root (eg - sudo alacarte in a terminal) but thats fixed now.

### Post by techno-mole on 2010-02-20
curious that your system wont run compiz, it seems powerful enough to run it, i dont know anything about ati graphics cards, but it sounds like it would be fine, again curious.

my system specs are - 

athalon x2 6000+ dual core running at 3.1ghz (stock speed, not over clocked)
4gb ddr2 ram (@800mhz, again not over clocked)
nvidia 9500gt pci express (512mb ddr3 ram)
2 sata hard drives, 1 x 500gb for ubuntu and 1 x 250gb for xp (i dual boot)
and i have a cmedia sound card, it does the job.

i have run compiz on much less, one of the systems in the house uses a single core athalon 2.1ghz cpu, 2gb of ram and an old nvidia fx5200 agp card, that runs compiz pretty well.

although this higlights the problem of uses a window manager etc by default, not all systems will run it well, some not at all, which is why there should be an option for using it or not.

for example when i first install karmic effects are of (until i install the restricted drivers)

so what if that by default all effects are off, so basic gnome panels etc, this way most systems should run ubuntu/gnome (unless i missed something ?)

then under the effects tab (under appearances) there could be a few options, 
1 - no effects, basic panels etc 

2 - gnome shell, when selected the system could then install anything needed, or enable whats needed to run gs etc etc 
and finally 

3 - run compiz, perhaps this should then have a low,medium and high options for the extent of the effects (basic effects or full whistles and bells etc)

each respective option would need some descriptions, maybe mention that each option will require better hardware, and such like.

i like gnome shell, but i dont think that it should be enabled by default, the same goes for compiz etc, it may well send people away, look at vista, wasnt it meant to be the dogs b's ? a lot of people realized that it is a dog of an os, no operating system in my book should use that much of a systems resources to simply run it, let alone do anything constructive with it, just my 2 pence

I've got a samsung r510 laptop. With dualcore 2,26 ghz and a Geforce 9200M GS. The problem isn't the CPU load it's the overal temperature of the laptop.

With gnome-shell on my videocard (GPU) is 97 degrees Celsius, and the other termometers indicated around 80-90 degrees celsius. I don't think it's all due too gnome-shell because when I turn gnome-shell off it still indicates 83 degrees. For as far as I know that is still too hot.

It's hard to find any information/solution regarding this problem :(

### Post by techno-mole on 2010-02-21
ive had a look at the cooling system for that particular laptop, at least the cpu cooler anyway.

i would still give it a good clean, if you know how to take it apart that is, although the various coolers should be easy enough to get to, just a case of removing a cover or 2.

if you cant get into the cooling fans get yourself a can of air ([http://www.maplin.co.uk/Module.aspx?ModuleNo=25351](http://www.maplin.co.uk/Module.aspx?ModuleNo=25351)) and give the vents a quick blast of that, all the vents you can find.

the other thing that normally causes laptops to over heat is having them on your lap, or on a bed etc, or any place where air cant circulate around the under side, ideally the laptop should be used on a flat, even surface so air can get underneath it, 
or you could get something like this - [http://www.maplin.co.uk/Module.aspx?ModuleNo=221694]("http://www.maplin.co.uk/Module.aspx?ModuleNo=221694")  or this [http://www.maplin.co.uk/Module.aspx?ModuleNo=221693]("http://www.maplin.co.uk/Module.aspx?ModuleNo=221693")

i cant think of any other reason why it would be over heating, is the graphics card a shared (eg - uses some of the system ram) or not ? maybe giving the system more ram ? 

maybe its just one of those things, which again highlights why things like compiz,gnome shell etc shouldnt be enabled by default as they could cause issues for people.

### Post by Roasted on 2010-02-22
> **NCLI said:**
> Try forcing yourself to use it without a dock for a few days, I'm zipping around with pure GS now, with no issues whatsoever.


I have. In fact, I eventually thought maybe I was just being kind of a stubborn ******* in regard to it when a few users here began saying the same thing to me, so I forced myself to use Gnome Shell exclusively for 30 days without the use of a dock. I made it about 3 weeks before I seriously wanted to punch every toddler nearby.

It's not that I don't think Gnome Shell is user friendly, but I feel as though the entire experience is hindered extensively by the lack of some sort of window or application switcher. I consider myself a power user. I multitask. A lot. So for me to continually have to hot corner to the upper left is a total PITA when maybe I'm sick of zooming in/out all of the time. Why can't I just snap over with the mouse and single click to my application?

I feel as though the overview is very, very solid. It's a great way to manage open windows. But when I use it as my soul means of switching through active applications, it tends to make me sick after 10 or 15 minutes.

And yes, I've tried to adapt to alt-tab. Didn't fly. I really just think they need an option, some sort of feature we can enable in the preferences to allow the many users (it's about a 10 to 3 ratio from users who want it vs users who don't want it, from what I've seen) to fill in the gap we'd like to see filled.

But fortunately the notification system is no longer activated by hitting the bottom of the screen, and only activated by hot cornering to the right, therefore making my auto-hide Docky2 compliment Gnome Shell VERY nicely, in my opinion.

Wouldn't use GS without it. Ever. So at least if GS lands without an integrated solution, I'll be quite happy to fire up Docky2. I just feel as though it makes GS feel very incomplete to have this feeling of installing a dock to make it feel complete. It kind of chips out a -1 to GS in my book. But it's not over yet - many months to go. Much faith is still had. We'll see. :)

---

### Post by Roasted on 2010-02-22
> **moore.bryan said:**
> 

Interesting take, Roasted. One of the reasons why I *stopped* using Gnome-Do's Docky was *because* GnomeShell's "pull-out" made it redundant.

I'm not sure what you mean, exactly. When you say Gnome Shell's pull-out, do you mean how it zooms out to the overview?

I said in my last post that while I think the overview is awesome, to continually zoom in/out just to simply switch an application multiplied by how many times a minute multiplied by how many times an hour, etc etc, it didn't fly well with me. I preferred to use the overview as a way to manage active applications and a way to manage workspaces - not as a way to navigate through open windows. It just didn't feel... right.

And once I tried the PPA of Docky2, I found it was quite a bit better than the Docky that was integrated in Gnome-Do, so I was sold. :) 

I'm not saying Gnome Shell must be changed to cater to me. I'm just one of quite a large quantity of people who believe the same thing. Just integrate a feature to allow us to activate a docky-like feature. It would make more sense, in my mind. I just don't see how having to use Docky or AWN or anything else with Gnome Shell is the "answer" to completing its user experience. I just think -something- needs to be integrated. 

It's kind of like buying a new computer. You buy it and you're like, pfft, look at all of this stupid bloatware. Let me uninstall this, and this, and this, and this, to get my computer use-able without all of this garbage starting up. In my mind, to fire up Gnome Shell and think, ahh wait, I gotta get a dock so it has a more sensible way of switching through active applications......... I don't see the logic in that. It'd be one thing if it was me vs the world, but I've seen the majority of users voice the same opinion I have. I'm just a little more headstrong about voicing it.

### Post by seeker5528 on 2010-02-22
> **OlyPerson said:**
> Basically what Merk said: there is no choice and many people's computers simply cannot use compositing.  I believe that's why it won't work with virtual machines either, but I'm not 100% sure if that's the reason.

I'm a bit skeptical that the number of people that can't use compositing is actually all that high.

Requiring people to have working OpenGL support in their hardware/driver combo for compositing to work is another story. The number of people with unacceptable OpenGL performance is significantly higher than the number of people with unacceptable Xrender performance.

Of course what is or isn't acceptable performance is a bit subjective and I don't know what the Xrender support is like when running in a VM.

Later, Seeker

### Post by ndefontenay on 2010-02-23
Hi guys,

this just came today on the mailing list. Lots lots lots of improvements.

> GNOME Shell 2.29.0 is now available at:

[http://ftp.gnome.org/pub/GNOME/sources/gnome-shell/2.29](http://ftp.gnome.org/pub/GNOME/sources/gnome-shell/2.29)

7429cd9689e1a2e302b1c23e06c576ee6b0542a7f1c72c4c0efce3d83842aa58 gnome-shell-2.29.0.tar.bz2
bc1dd6426c59e4a93d2768c408d5b34181aa722d5e83ea4c811c1eb19261e3f5 gnome-shell-2.29.0.tar.gz

Note on Clutter requirement: Due to problems encountered in testing 
  GNOME Shell against Clutter 1.1, gnome-shell-2.29.0 still requires
  Clutter 1.0. We expect to have the problems in Clutter and gnome-shell
  resolved before the release of gnome-shell-2.29.1, and switch over
  to requiring Clutter 1.1 for that.

About GNOME Shell
=================

GNOME Shell provides core user interface functions for the GNOME 3
desktop, like switching to windows and launching applications. 
GNOME Shell takes advantage of the capabilities of modern graphics
hardware and introduces innovative user interface concepts to 
provide a visually attractive and easy to use experience.

This is still a VERY EARLY version of GNOME Shell and should not be
taken of indicative of the final user interface or performance.

We are providing tarball releases at this point largely to allow
distributions to start building packaging for GNOME Shell. If you are
interested in building GNOME Shell from source, we would recommend
building from version control using the build script described at:

[http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell](http://live.gnome.org/GnomeShell)

Not only will that give you the very latest version of this rapidly
changing project, it will be much easier than get GNOME Shell and
its dependencies to build from tarballs.

Changes since 2.28.0
====================

* New message tray showing notifications sliding into the bottom of the screen.
  Includes a status area for past notifications, and also pulls in "urgent
  window" handled in GNOME 2 by flashing the taskbar. 
  [Dan, Marina, Florian M, Adel, Jon N]
* Add the ability to set your presence to the user status menu [Dan]
* Allow switching the overview between a grid and linear view of workspaces [Maxim]
* Redo the application browser to use a grid of icons [Maxim]
* Initial version of the extension system [Colin, Siegfried]
* Initial support for RTL layout in ST and the shell [Abderrahim]
* Add undo capability to overview, e.g., when removing favorites [Maxim]
* New workspace switcher popup [Adel, Mads]

* Work on visual appearance to match mockups [Adel, Colin, Florian M]
* Port most of existing code to CSS [Colin]
* Other visual improvements
  - Show a scaled-up excerpt from the application item in the panel [Colin]
  - Minimize windows to 'Activities' button [Adel]
  - Fade out desktop icons when showing overview =Florian M]
  - Use a fixed ordering for well-known icons in the system tray [Colin]
  - Nicer animation of hidden windows when transitioning to/from the overview [Leon]
  - Draw a ripple when the hot corner is hit [Owen - collaboratively hacked in a MIT IAP session]

* App switcher (Alt-Tab) improvements
  - Many behavior and visual refinements based on design feedback [Dan]
  - Show a separator between windows on this and other workspaces [Steve]
  - Position on the currently focused monitor [Adel]
  - Bug fixes [Steve]

* Miscellaneous behavioral improvements
  - Allow workspace switching by mousewheel on indicators in overview ]Adel]
  - Allow dropping on workspace indicators [Adel]
  - Enable DND of search results [Florian M]
  - When closing a window from the overview that puts up a confirm dialog,
    switch to that dialog [Florian M]
  - When the sidebar is collapsed, auto-hide it [Florian S]
  - Show "No matching results" when search matches nothing [Colin]
  - Limit user to 16 workspaces [Dan]
  - Allow clicking on any part of section headers in the dash for browsing [Florian M]
  - Match on executable names as well when searching [Florian M]
  - For clock AM/PM vs. 24-hour based on the locale ['res']

* ST (Shell Toolkit) improvements
- CSS addtions:
    . width/height [Steve]
    . max-width/max-height [Dan]
    . -st-shadow [Florian M]
    . gradients [Colin, Florian M]
    . radial gradients [Florian M]
    . Non-uniform border widths (when no corner radius) [Colin]
    . text-decoration: [underline|strikethrough] [Colin]
- Make allocation behavior consistent across actors [Dan]
- Support fixed-position children in StBoxLayout [Colin]
- Support raising and lowering children in StBoxLayout [Colin]
- Add st_box_layout_move_child and st_box_layout_insert_actor [Marina]
- Add st_box_layout_remove_all, st_box_layout_destroy_children, st_box_layout_get_n_children [Colin]
- Add StClickable, StOverflowBox actors [Colin]
- Support setting scrollbar policy on StScrollView [Colin]
- Support shadows on edges of StScrollView to hint at more content [Maxim]

* Make places results available in search [JP]
* Handle changes to the current resolution [Maxim, Florian M]
* Bug fixes for multihead support [Florian S, Siegfried]
* Make screen shell recorder work again [Owen, Jon N]

* More miscellaneous improvements
- Add gnome-shell --eval-file=<file> to eval file in running shell via D-BUS [Colin]
- Add eject buttons to places section [Florian M]
- Add completion to the Alt-F2 run dialog [Maxim]
- Make screen recording parameters configurable [Adel]
- Add an initial version of a manpage [Marcelo]

* Performance improvements [Colin, Florian M]
* Code cleanups [Adel, Colin, Dan, Marina, Owen]
* Bug fixes [Adel, Carlos, Colin, Dan, Florian M, Gustavo, Jon M, JP, Maxim, 
  Nickolas, Owen, Siegfried, Steve]
* Build fixes [Andre, Colin, Dan, Javier, Marina, Maxim, Owen, Richard]

Code Contributors:
  Maxim Ermilov, Steve Frécinaux, Adel Gadllah, Siegfried-Angel Gevatter Pujals,
  Leon Handreke, Richard Hughes, Abderrahim Kitouni, Andre Klapper, Javier Jardón,
  Nickolas Lloyd, William Jon McCann, Florian Müllner, Carlos Martín Nieto, Jon Nettleton,
  JP St. Pierre, Gustavo Noronha Silva, Owen Taylor, Marcelo Jorge Vieira, 
  Mads Villadsen, Colin Walters, Dan Winship, Marina Zhurakhinskaya, 'res'

Design:
  William Jon McCann, Jeremy Perry (and others)

Translations:
  Khaled Hosny (ar), Ivaylo Valkov (bg), Siegfried-Angel Gevatter Pujals (ca),
  Petr Kovar (cz), Mario Blättermann (de), Kris Thomsen (dk), Jorge González (es),
  Timo Jyrinki (fi), Claude Paroz (fr), Antón Méixome (gl), Jennie Petoumenou (gr),
  Liel Fridman (he), Gabor Kelemen (hu), Milo Casagrande (it), Kjartan Maraas (nb),
  A S Alam (pa), Amanda Magalhães (pt_BR), Lucian Grijincu (ro),
  Sergey V. Kovylov, Andrey Korzinev, Marina Zhurakhinskaya (ru), Milo&#353; Popovi&#263; (sb),
  Daniel Nylander (se), Matej Urban&#269;i&#269; (sl), Nguy&#7877;n Thái Ng&#7885;c Duy (vi), 'jiero' (zh_CN)

Bugs fixed:

571109 Handle minimized windows better when switching to/from overlay
582229 Warnings everytime app monitor saves active apps
582655 associate windows per application in workspace view
582763 Maximising windows can make them very blocky as they "grow"
584526 Doesn't handle resolution changes
585473 BigRectangle: Use pre-multiplied colors
587069 Move the selection to the first search result on search and perform queued searches when the user hits Enter
588056 Add xterm and x11-apps as dependency in Debian and Ubuntu
590686 Can alt-Tab out of grab
591854 Removing all favorites makes it impossible to add new ones
591866 Implement new search design
591912 background doesn't show when nautilus isn't drawing the desktop
592024 dash: Make recent docs display two columns
593844 Alternative overview design idea
594556 Workspace indicator should darken for app-well menu
594671 improve new workspace button
595321 memory leaks (ongoing)
596264 2.28 tracker

### Post by icyzer on 2010-02-23
I'm wondering. Has anyone found out how to edit the files as such to make the panel transparent? That would be awesome, I could set up conky to show the hardware info. Any ideas?
I tryed some stuff myself but I'm not knowledged in the field of programming :)

---

### Post by xtoudi on 2010-02-23
> **icyzer said:**
> I'm wondering. Has anyone found out how to edit the files as such to make the panel transparent? That would be awesome, I could set up conky to show the hardware info. Any ideas?
I tryed some stuff myself but I'm not knowledged in the field of programming :)

file:
~/gnome-shell/source/gnome-shell/data/theme/gnome-shell.css (if GIT version, I don't know where is this file located in case of PPA)


```
#panel {
    color: #ffffff;
    font-size: 16px;
    background-gradient-direction: vertical;
    background-color: rgba(0,0,0,0.98);
}
```

it gives nice vertical gradient top panel.

and ... just search is what you need ;-) (this thread)

### Post by mohitchawla on 2010-02-23
I just upgraded to the 2.29 version using the Ricotz testing PPA. There are a few problems I noticed right away. 

First, there were no applications listed in the Applications menu(the main menu, not the space for favorites/currently opened apps). It was empty. Restarted gdm, restarted system, no go. Then I just launched a .htm file to be able to use Mozilla, a text file, a media file and from then on, applications are listed in the menu. So, that's sorted.

Second is, the window transitions are NOT smooth at all. Jittery & slow. They were fine in the previous version. 
I am not sure if this has got something to do with the Ricotz PPA build.
Hope to find the solution for this.

---

### Post by techno-mole on 2010-02-23
> 


```
#panel {
    color: #ffffff;
    font-size: 16px;
    background-gradient-direction: vertical;
    background-color: rgba(0,0,0,0.98);
}
```

it gives nice vertical gradient top panel.



ive been messing around with the css and the svg images, i just tried the css code you gave, my panel was mostly black ?

mine looks like this - 
```
#panel {
    color: #ffffff;
    font-size: 16px;
    background-gradient-direction: vertical;
    background-gradient-start: #6E6E6E;
    background-gradient-end: #151515;
    border-bottom: 1px solid #1C1C1C;
}
```

this gives a grey panel, moving from a lightish shade to a darkish shade (vertically) 

i have tried adding various things to see what happens, i did try some css transparency, but it didnt work.
im thinking that transparency might be more to do with the compositing than the css.

the icons used in gnome shell can easily be changed using inkscape, colours,shapes etc you can get some interesting results, just make sure you keep the original names of svg files.

im thinking of making some theme packs that can be used with gnome shell, im not sure where the devs are going with theming, but this might lead to some interesting themes being created.

---

### Post by Arlanthir on 2010-02-23
> **techno-mole said:**
> ive been messing around with the css and the svg images, i just tried the css code you gave, my panel was mostly black ?

mine looks like this - 
```
#panel {
    color: #ffffff;
    font-size: 16px;
    background-gradient-direction: vertical;
    background-gradient-start: #6E6E6E;
    background-gradient-end: #151515;
    border-bottom: 1px solid #1C1C1C;
}
```

this gives a grey panel, moving from a lightish shade to a darkish shade (vertically) 

i have tried adding various things to see what happens, i did try some css transparency, but it didnt work.
im thinking that transparency might be more to do with the compositing than the css.

the icons used in gnome shell can easily be changed using inkscape, colours,shapes etc you can get some interesting results, just make sure you keep the original names of svg files.

im thinking of making some theme packs that can be used with gnome shell, im not sure where the devs are going with theming, but this might lead to some interesting themes being created.

Did you try the suggested ```
background-color: rgba(0,0,0,0.98);
``` in particular?
I think that is the important part and it seems you omitted it :P

### Post by techno-mole on 2010-02-23
here is a screenshot, it is very subtle, which is why i missed it the first time.

its not quite right though (in my opinion) for me it makes some of the tray icons look like they are not quite inline with the rest of the panel, for my own preference i prefer the icons to have an equal amount of panel top and bottom, so they look in line (where possible)

and although it is transparent, its not quite the same as you can do with the gnome panels, or other methods, maybe its something they are working on ?

again gnome shell has only just gone alpha (i think) so im not expecting much, just trying stuff out.

one thing though is that some times if i right click tray icons they are partly covered by the panel, so im thinking auto hiding might be a feature thats needed, it is for me as i like to not have panels,docks showing, unless i need them.

```
background-color: rgba(0,0,0,0.98);
```
This is RGBA - the last value (0.98 ) is the alpha channel value - try with this - you can do anything you want. Additional you can delete lines with "gradient" - then you have solid panel or .... solid transparent .... or full transparent.

### Post by ranch hand on 2010-02-24
OK, I have curl, I have jhbuild.  I go to gnome live and follow those instructions in a chroot environment to my ISO testing install.  i get this message:
```

root@sam-desktop:/# /bin/bash gnome-shell-build-setup.sh
GNOME 2.26 or newer is required to build GNOME Shell

```
This is going to a fresh install of A3.  I thought we were newer than 2.26.

What do I do?

### Post by Merk42 on 2010-02-24
I'm not familiar with chroot, never used it as it's not needed for GNOME Shell.
When building from source GNOME Shell resides in the $home folder, so maybe you're making it more complicated than it needs to be and that's why it's not working?

### Post by ranch hand on 2010-02-24
Chroot is just like running in terminal from the OS you are chrooted to (at least I believe so in my noobish way - it sure acts like it).

I checked here and we are on 2.29.91.

Oh well, I,ll try it from there and see what happens.

Thanks a bunch.

### Post by ranch hand on 2010-02-24
OK, I went over there and ran every thing.  It is not something that should be done in chroot anyway as the permissions are going to be wrong if you are running as root.  I was also chrooted to my root partition and needed to be to the home partition.

Every thing ran very easily using the script provided on gnome live.

The very last line of the output was;
```

PATH does not contain /home/ike/bin, it is recommended that you add that.

```
I do have a vague idea what this means but have no idea how to proceed.  I sure hope some kind soul will let me know.

### Post by Reiger on 2010-02-24
Well I think it's the chrooting thing playing tricks on you. 'Cause this is part of my .profile:
```

# set PATH so it includes user's private bin if it exists
if [ -d "$HOME/bin" ] ; then
    PATH="$HOME/bin:$PATH"
fi

```

And that file is unmodified (IOW: the default). But of course that only gets loaded if I log on to my account here.

If $HOME is set appropriately in the chroot then you could inline the .profile script like this:

```

cd /path/to/home/
# reload profile
# you can fix $HOME by issuing: export $HOME="/path/to/home"; 
. .profile
echo "continue working here"

```

### Post by houseworkshy on 2010-02-24
From what I've skimmed through above, it's a long thread, I hope that gnome shell will be an option not a compulsory default.  I have several reasons for saying this.  
The current GNOME is an easy environment which people who are not tech minded can find their way around whilst at the same time there is nothing that one can't do in it if one wishes.  
I know several new converts to Linux who were won over by gnomes simplicity and, how do I phrase this, are at the stage in life where learning new things is difficult and off putting.  
Though it is mostly, not all, eye candy compiz has done much to win over the younger end of the market, people even post screen captures on youtube to show how much cooler their desktops are than the commercial alternatives.

This is not a flame, good luck to the gnome shell team and more power to your elbow.  There are at least a couple of dozen GUI's and as far as I'm concerned the more the merrier.  Just please Ubuntu don't make it the default, given a few hours and some reading up I can switch my GUI, most users, such as the huge crowd of application only users fleeing the unsupported XP, can't.  Changing the GUI just as Ubuntu is entering the mainstream will severely dampen it's popularity.  
In fact as a general principle radical overhauls are only a good idea if they are optional.  Many people just want to use their computers for business and recreation and are only interested in applications.  They learn what security they are forced to learn and no more.  Years of bugs, viruses and various computer problems which have lost valuable data, work and time have conditioned them out of any enthusiasm for the inner workings of operating systems. Most non-geeks actually loath compulsory changes as much as buggy releases because they simply want to get on with the things they want to do. I suspect the majority of Ubuntu users are non-geeks and as such lots of them will have what amounts to a phobia of all the things geeks love.

IMO
Extra option = great
compulsory = very bad

---
